data.detailed_description: The Palace of Westminster, home to Big Ben and UK Parliament, is one of London's most iconic landmarks. The current neo-Gothic building was completed in 1870 after the Great Fire of 1834. The Elizabeth Tower (Big Ben) stands 96 meters tall and houses the world's second-largest four-faced chiming clock. The Great Bell weighs over 13 tonnes and chimes every hour with impeccable precision.
data.history: Originally built for Edward the Confessor over 1,000 years ago, it remained a royal residence for 400 years before becoming Britain's administrative center. The current building, designed by Sir Charles Barry and Augustus Pugin, was completed in 1870. The tower was renamed Elizabeth Tower in 2012 to commemorate Queen Elizabeth II's Diamond Jubilee.
tips: Must be 11+ to tour - requires climbing 334 steps with no lift
Book 2-3 weeks in advance as tours sell out quickly

Not suitable for mobility issues, heart conditions, or fear of heights

data.detailed_description: The world's most visited museum, housing over 380,000 objects and displaying 35,000 works across 72,735 square meters. The collection spans from ancient civilizations to 19th-century masterpieces. Must-sees include the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and Winged Victory of Samothrace. The iconic glass pyramid entrance was added in 1989. One of the most prestigious cultural institutions globally.
data.history: Built as a fortress in the late 12th century under Philip II, the Louvre became a royal palace in the 16th century under Francis I. It opened as a public museum during the French Revolution in 1793 with 537 paintings. Napoleon greatly expanded the collection with spoils from his military campaigns. The modern Louvre spans eight centuries of French history.
tips: Book timed-entry tickets online to skip 2+ hour queues
Download Louvre app for free audio guides and navigation
Start early with must-sees: Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, Winged Victory

data.detailed_description: Fushimi Inari Shrine is dedicated to Inari, the Shinto god of rice and prosperity. Famous for thousands of vermillion torii gates forming tunnels along mountain trails. The iconic torii path leads 4km up Mount Inari (233m). Each gate is donated by individuals/businesses - donor names inscribed on back. Full hike takes 2-3 hours with panoramic Kyoto views from summit.
data.history: Founded in 711 AD, making it one of Kyoto's oldest and most important shrines. The torii gate donation tradition began in the Edo period and continues today. Over 10,000 torii gates now line the mountain. Inari shrines are the most common type in Japan - over 30,000 nationwide, with Fushimi Inari as the head shrine.
tips: Visit before 8am to avoid tour groups and get empty gate photos
Wear comfortable walking shoes - steep stairs and uneven paths
Bring water and snacks - limited vendors on mountain
Full trail is 4km round trip
Don't need to hike to summit - lower gates equally beautiful
Free unlike many Kyoto temples
facilities: Restrooms at base and along trail

data.detailed_description: The Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheatre) is ancient Rome's most iconic landmark and the world's largest amphitheater. Built 72-80 AD, it held 65,000 spectators for gladiatorial contests and public spectacles. The elliptical structure stands 48m high with a complex underground hypogeum where gladiators and animals prepared. Your ticket includes the Roman Forum and Palatine Hill.
data.history: Emperor Vespasian commissioned construction in 72 AD, financed by plunder from Jerusalem. His son Titus opened it in 80 AD with 100 days of games costing 5,000 animal lives. Emperor Domitian later added underground spaces and an extra story, making it the largest Roman amphitheater. It operated for over 500 years until the 6th century.

data.detailed_description: Central Park spans 843 acres (59th-110th St) in the heart of Manhattan. America's first public park, designed 1858 by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux. Features meadows, lakes, gardens, woodlands, a reservoir, plus iconic landmarks like Bethesda Fountain, Bow Bridge, Belvedere Castle. Hosts 42 million visitors annually - one of the world's most visited urban parks.
data.history: Designed in the mid-19th century as a public park for all New Yorkers regardless of background or income. Construction began in 1857, transforming rocky swampland into a landscaped masterpiece. Opened in 1858 and completed in 1873. Designated a National Historic Landmark in 1963. The Conservancy began major restoration in 1980s, transforming the park from decline to glory.

Highlights include the Rosetta Stone, Egyptian mummies, Parthenon Sculptures (Elgin Marbles), Sutton Hoo treasures. The Great Court with its glass roof is a architectural masterpiece. Completely free entry to permanent collection.
data.history: Founded in 1753 based on the collections of Sir Hans Sloane. Opened to public in 1759 - one of the first museums worldwide. Originally housed in Montagu House, the current building was constructed from 1823 onwards. The iconic Reading Room (1857) hosted Marx, Lenin, and Virginia Woolf. The Great Court addition (2000) by Norman Foster enclosed Europe's largest covered square.

data.detailed_description: One of the Seven Natural Wonders of the World. The canyon is 277 miles long, up to 18 miles wide, and over a mile deep (6,000 ft). Carved by the Colorado River over 6 million years, exposing 2 billion years of Earth's geological history. The South Rim is most accessible (90% of visitors), open year-round with extensive facilities. North Rim is more remote, higher elevation (8,000 ft), open seasonally.
data.history: Native Americans inhabited the canyon for thousands of years (Pueblo, Hopi, Navajo, Paiute). Spanish explorer García López de Cárdenas was the first European to view it (1540). John Wesley Powell led the first Colorado River expedition (1869). Declared a National Monument in 1908 by Theodore Roosevelt, became a National Park in 1919. UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1979.
tips: Stay hydrated - bring 1 gallon water per person per day
Arrive before 10am or after 4pm to avoid peak crowds/parking issues

Don't hike down and back up in one day - dangerous for unprepared hikers

data.detailed_description: The Acropolis is ancient Athens' sacred citadel on a rocky outcrop 150m above the city. The crown jewel is the Parthenon (447-432 BC), a temple dedicated to goddess Athena, featuring 46 outer Doric columns. Also includes the Erechtheion with its famous Caryatids (female column figures), the Temple of Athena Nike, and the Propylaea entrance gate. Symbol of democracy and Western civilization.
data.history: The hill has been inhabited since 4000 BC. During the Golden Age of Athens (5th century BC), Pericles initiated the construction of the Parthenon and other buildings. The Parthenon served as a temple, treasury, fortress, and church over millennia. It suffered major damage in 1687 when Venetians bombed Ottoman ammunition stored inside. Lord Elgin removed many sculptures (1801-1812), now in the British Museum (controversial).
tips: Wear comfortable, non-slip shoes - marble surfaces very slippery
Bring water, sunscreen, hat - little shade available
Arrive right at opening (8am) to beat tour groups

Over 2 million visitors annually make it Amsterdam's 2nd most popular museum after the Rijksmuseum.

data.detailed_description: Paris's most iconic landmark, standing 330m (1,083 ft) tall. Built for the 1889 World's Fair celebrating the French Revolution's centennial. Named after engineer Gustave Eiffel. Features three levels accessible by elevator/stairs: 1st (57m), 2nd (115m), and summit (276m). The iron lattice structure weighs 10,100 tons. Illuminated nightly with 20,000 light bulbs. Sparkles for 5 minutes every hour on the hour.
data.history: Designed by Gustave Eiffel and completed in 1889 for the Exposition Universelle. Originally planned as a temporary structure for 20 years. Criticized by many Parisians initially ('metal asparagus'). Saved from demolition by its usefulness for radio transmission. Has become the universal symbol of Paris. Painted every 7 years (60 tons of paint). Over 7 million visitors annually make it the world's most-visited paid monument.

data.detailed_description: Antoni Gaudí's masterpiece and Barcelona's most iconic landmark. This UNESCO World Heritage basilica has been under construction since 1882, expected to complete by 2026. Features soaring spires, intricate facades (Nativity and Passion), and a stunning interior flooded with colorful light through elaborate stained glass. The organic, nature-inspired design combines Gothic and Art Nouveau styles. The basilica will have 18 towers when complete - 12 for apostles, 4 for evangelists, 1 for Mary, 1 for Jesus.
data.history: Construction began in 1882 by Francisco de Paula del Villar. Gaudí took over in 1883, transforming the design with his unique vision. He devoted his last 15 years exclusively to the project until his death in 1926 (hit by tram). The Spanish Civil War halted progress and destroyed many plans. Construction resumed in the 1950s and continues today using Gaudí's models and modern technology. It became a basilica in 2010 when Pope Benedict XVI consecrated it.

Download Sagrada Família app before visit (free audioguide in 19 languages)

data.detailed_description: Prehistoric monument of massive standing stones arranged in a ring. Built in several stages between 3000-2000 BC. The stones weigh up to 25 tons and were transported from Wales (240 miles away). Purpose remains debated - astronomical observatory, healing temple, or burial site. UNESCO World Heritage Site. The visitor center features Neolithic houses reconstruction, interactive exhibits, and 360 displays. Free shuttle takes you 1 mile to the stone circle.
data.history: Construction began around 3000 BC during the Neolithic period. The massive sarsen stones were erected around 2500 BC, transported from Marlborough Downs (20 miles). The smaller bluestones came from Wales' Preseli Hills (140 miles). Used continuously for over 1500 years for ceremonies and burials. Abandoned around 1600 BC. Became a tourist attraction in the 18th century. Now managed by English Heritage.

data.detailed_description: Preserved hiding place where Anne Frank and 7 others hid from Nazis for 2+ years (1942-1944). The Secret Annex is accessed through the famous revolving bookcase. Original building with no furniture (removed during war) but powerful atmosphere and original items. Anne's diary on display. Museum tells story through quotes, photos, videos. data.history: The building dates from 1635. Otto Frank's business occupied the front in 1940. The Frank family (parents + Anne + Margot), Van Pels family, and Fritz Pfeffer hid here from July 1942. Betrayed and arrested August 1944. Anne died in Bergen-Belsen concentration camp in early 1945, age 15. Otto (sole survivor) returned and found Anne's diary preserved by Miep Gies. Published 1947. Museum opened 1960, saved from demolition by public campaign.

data.detailed_description: World's largest ancient castle complex (70,000 m²) and official residence of Czech presidents. Founded in 9th century, it's a stunning mix of Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance, and Baroque architecture. Main sights: St. Vitus Cathedral (Gothic masterpiece with Mucha windows), Old Royal Palace, Golden Lane (tiny colorful houses), St. George's Basilica. Offers panoramic Prague views. Changing of the Guard ceremony every hour.
data.history: Founded around 880 AD by Prince Bořivoj as a fortified settlement. Became seat of Czech kings, Holy Roman Emperors, and presidents. Charles IV expanded it in 14th century, adding St. Vitus Cathedral. Habsburg rulers added Renaissance/Baroque elements. Defenestration of Prague (1618) started Thirty Years' War here. Communist era saw it as government seat. Václav Havel made it symbolic of democracy after 1989.

data.detailed_description: Fairy-tale 19th century Romanesque Revival palace commissioned by 'Mad' King Ludwig II. Perched dramatically on a rugged hill above Hohenschwangau village with Alpine backdrop. Inspiration for Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle. Lavishly decorated interiors with themes from Wagner operas. Only 14 of 200 rooms completed before Ludwig's mysterious death. Reached by 30-40 min uphill walk, shuttle bus, or horse carriage. Marienbrücke (Mary's Bridge) offers best photo views.
data.history: Built 1869-1886 by King Ludwig II of Bavaria as a retreat and homage to Richard Wagner. Ludwig lived here only 172 days before his mysterious drowning in 1886. Opened to public 7 weeks after his death to pay off debts. Over 61 million visitors since. Named after medieval knights. Design blends Romanesque, Gothic and Byzantine styles. Modern conveniences included central heating, running water, telephones.

data.transport_car: Scenic drives, but parking limited in mountain villages. Mountain passes closed in winter. Trains more practical
data.best_time_to_visit: Jul-Sep for hiking, wildflowers, warm weather. Dec-Mar for skiing. Jun and Oct for fewer crowds. Avoid Nov (many lifts closed)
data.detailed_description: The Swiss Alps are a major mountain range spanning Switzerland, featuring iconic peaks like Matterhorn (4,478m), Jungfrau, Eiger, and Mont Blanc. World-renowned for skiing, hiking, mountaineering, and stunning scenery. Key regions: Jungfrau Region (Interlaken, Grindelwald, Lauterbrunnen), Zermatt (Matterhorn), Lucerne/Engelberg (Mt. Titlis), St. Moritz. Features glaciers, pristine lakes, charming villages, mountain railways. Over 100,000km of hiking trails.
data.history: Formed 65-2 million years ago by collision of African and Eurasian tectonic plates. First Alpine crossing recorded 390 BC. Modern tourism began mid-1800s with Romantic movement celebrating nature. Golden Age of Alpinism: 1854-1865 saw first ascents of major peaks. Matterhorn conquered 1865. Mountain railways revolutionized access - Jungfrau Railway (1912) reached 3,454m. Now 7.5 million visitors annually.
tips: Swiss Travel Pass (3-15 days) offers best value - trains, buses, boats included
Weather changes rapidly - bring layers even in summer
Book accommodations well ahead in peak season (Jul-Aug, Dec-Feb)
Jungfraujoch (Top of Europe) often cloudy - check webcams before going
Many mountain lifts close Nov and Apr-May for maintenance
Drinking water from fountains is safe throughout

data.opening_hours: Island open year-round. Most businesses: Apr-Oct. Archaeological sites: 8am-3pm (winter), 8am-8pm (summer)
data.admission_adult: Akrotiri ruins: €12. Ancient Thera: €6. Most attractions free (churches, beaches, villages)
data.admission_notes: Caldera views and villages FREE to explore. Ferry/flight to reach island
data.transport_train: N/A (island)
data.transport_bus: KTEL buses connect villages (€2-3). Fira is main hub. Frequent to Oia, Perissa, Kamari
data.transport_car: ATV/car rental €20-50/day. Essential for flexibility. Parking difficult in Oia/Fira. Narrow winding roads
data.best_time_to_visit: Apr-May or Sep-Oct (warm, fewer crowds, cheaper). Jun-Aug very crowded/expensive. Oia sunset packed Jul-Aug. Winter (Nov-Mar) many places closed
data.detailed_description: Stunning volcanic island in the Aegean Sea, part of the Cyclades. Famous for whitewashed cubic houses with blue domes perched on cliffs above the caldera. Formed by volcanic eruption ~3,600 years ago that may have inspired Atlantis legend. Main towns: Fira (capital), Oia (sunset views, most photographed), Imerovigli. Volcanic black/red sand beaches. Ancient Akrotiri Minoan ruins (Bronze Age Pompeii). World-famous sunsets, wine, unique volcanic landscape.
data.history: Inhabited since 3000 BC. Major Minoan civilization settlement. Massive volcanic eruption around 1600 BC destroyed the island center, created caldera, and possibly caused Minoan civilization's decline on Crete. Island resettled by Phoenicians, then Greeks. Venetians ruled 1204-1579, adding fortifications. Ottoman rule 1579-1821. Major earthquake 1956 destroyed many buildings - rebuilt in current white cubic style. Tourism boom since 1970s.
tips: Book accommodation well ahead (6+ months) for Jun-Aug
Rent ATV or car for flexibility - public buses crowded
Oia sunset viewpoint packed - arrive 1.5hrs early or skip crowds
Alternative sunset views: Imerovigli, Akrotiri Lighthouse
Visit Akrotiri ruins in morning before heat
Red Beach beautiful but pebbles/stones - bring water shoes

data.opening_hours: 30min before sunrise to 30min before sunset (approx 6am-7pm). Closed Fridays. Night viewing: 8:30pm-12:30am (full moon +2 days before/after)
data.admission_adult: ₹1,100 (~$13 USD) foreigners. ₹50 Indians. +₹200 for main mausoleum access
data.admission_notes: SAARC/BIMSTEC: ₹540. Online booking discount: ₹50 (foreigners), ₹5 (Indians). Foreign ticket includes water, shoe covers, guide map
data.transport_train: Train to Agra Cantt from Delhi (2-3hrs). Auto-rickshaw/taxi to Taj (15min, ₹100-200)
data.transport_bus: Buses from Delhi (ISBT Kashmere Gate) to Agra (4-5hrs)
data.transport_car: From Delhi: 230km via Yamuna Expressway (3-4hrs). Parking near gates
data.best_time_to_visit: Sunrise for best light and fewer crowds. Oct-Mar best weather. Avoid May-Jun extreme heat. Full moon night viewing magical but book months ahead
data.detailed_description: Iconic white marble mausoleum built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan for his wife Mumtaz Mahal. One of the New Seven Wonders of the World and UNESCO World Heritage Site. The perfectly symmetrical complex features a central dome (73m high), four minarets, beautiful gardens (Charbagh), and intricate pietra dura inlay work with precious stones. The marble changes color throughout the day - pinkish at dawn, white at noon, golden at sunset. Built 1632-1648 by 20,000 workers.
data.history: Commissioned in 1632 by Shah Jahan after his beloved wife Mumtaz Mahal died during childbirth. Designed by architect Ustad Ahmad Lahori. Construction took 22 years with 20,000 artisans and craftsmen from India, Persia, Ottoman Empire, and Europe. Over 1,000 elephants transported materials. Precious stones came from across Asia - jade (China), turquoise (Tibet), lapis lazuli (Afghanistan). Legend says Shah Jahan planned black marble mausoleum for himself across river. Designated UNESCO site 1983.
tips: Book tickets online for discount and avoid queues
Arrive at sunrise (6am) for best photos and small crowds
Enter from East Gate (shorter queues than West)
Main mausoleum extra ₹200 but worth it - limited to 400 people/day
Fridays closed except for mosque prayers (afternoon only)
No tripods, drones, or large bags allowed. Free shoe covers provided

data.opening_hours: Varies by section. Badaling: 6:30am-7pm (summer), 7:30am-6pm (winter). Mutianyu: 7:30am-5:30pm. Jinshanling: 7am-5pm
data.admission_adult: ¥40 ($6) Badaling. ¥45 Mutianyu. Cable car extra ¥100-140
data.admission_child: Half price (ages 6-18). Free under 6. Students with ID: half price
data.admission_notes: Must book online with ID. Bring passport. Cable car/chairlift separate. Toboggan slide ¥60 (Mutianyu)
data.transport_train: Badaling: High-speed train from Beijing North Railway Station (¥15, 1hr). Mutianyu: No direct train
data.transport_bus: Bus 877 from Deshengmen to Badaling (¥12, 1.5hrs). Private tour buses to Mutianyu/other sections
data.transport_car: Badaling: 70km from Beijing (1.5hrs). Mutianyu: 73km (2hrs). Parking available
data.visit_duration: 3-4 hours per section (half day with travel)
data.best_time_to_visit: Apr-May, Sep-Oct (good weather, fewer tourists). Avoid national holidays (Oct 1-7, Chinese New Year). Mutianyu less crowded than Badaling. Early morning best
data.detailed_description: Ancient fortification system stretching 21,196km across northern China. Built over 2,000+ years by various dynasties to protect against invasions. Most famous sections built by Ming Dynasty (1368-1644). Popular sections: Badaling (most visited, fully restored), Mutianyu (less crowded, cable car, toboggan), Jinshanling (hiking, watchtowers), Simatai (night viewing). Brick and stone wall follows mountain ridges with spectacular views.
data.history: Construction began 7th century BC during Warring States period. First Emperor Qin Shi Huang unified walls around 221 BC into single defense system. Ming Dynasty (1368-1644) rebuilt and extended walls with brick/stone - most visited sections today. Built by millions of workers over centuries, many died during construction. Fell into disrepair after Ming Dynasty. UNESCO World Heritage Site 1987. Restoration ongoing.
tips: Mutianyu recommended over Badaling (less crowded, better views)
Bring water, snacks - limited vendors on wall
Wear comfortable hiking shoes - steep steps, uneven surfaces
Badaling very crowded - arrive by 8am or visit other sections
Cable car recommended to save energy for walking on wall
Mutianyu toboggan slide down is fun alternative to cable car

data.opening_hours: Apr-Oct: 8:30am-5pm (last entry 4:10pm). Nov-Mar: 8:30am-4:30pm (last entry 3:40pm). Closed Mondays
data.admission_adult: ¥60 ($9) Apr-Oct. ¥40 Nov-Mar
data.admission_child: Free (under 18 with ID, students half price)
data.admission_notes: MUST book online with passport (no on-site sales). Audio guide ¥40. Treasure Gallery +¥10. Clock Gallery +¥10
data.transport_train: Metro Line 1 to Tiananmen East or Tiananmen West (5-10 min walk to entrance)
data.transport_bus: Bus 1, 2, 52, 82, 120 to Tiananmen East or West
data.transport_car: No parking at Forbidden City. Use public transport
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (8:30am) or late afternoon. Avoid weekends, holidays, May-Oct peak season. Tuesdays closed. Best weather: Apr-May, Sep-Oct
data.detailed_description: Imperial palace complex from Ming/Qing dynasties (1420-1912). World's largest palace complex with 980 buildings covering 72 hectares. Served as home to 24 emperors over 500 years. Features stunning traditional Chinese architecture with red walls, yellow glazed roof tiles, intricate carvings. Main structures: Hall of Supreme Harmony (largest wooden building), Imperial Garden, treasure galleries. 1 million+ artifacts. UNESCO World Heritage Site 1987.
data.history: Built 1406-1420 under Ming Emperor Yongle. Took 1 million workers and 100,000 artisans 14 years to complete. Served as political/ceremonial center and home to emperors until 1912 when Last Emperor Puyi abdicated. Forbidden to commoners - hence the name. Opened as Palace Museum in 1925. Suffered damage during Japanese invasion and Cultural Revolution but extensively restored. Now receives 19 million visitors annually (most visited museum in world).
tips: MUST book online with passport details in advance - no on-site tickets
Enter from south (Meridian Gate), exit north (Shenwu Gate)
Rent audio guide (¥40) for historical context - essential
Wear comfortable shoes - massive complex with lots of walking
Avoid Mondays (closed) and weekends/holidays (packed)
Treasure/Clock galleries require extra tickets but worthwhile

data.opening_hours: 5am-6pm daily (year-round). Temple complex open all days. Ticket office: 4:30am-5:30pm
data.admission_adult: $37 (1-day), $62 (3-day valid 10 days), $72 (7-day valid 30 days)
data.admission_notes: Buy online or at ticket office. Tickets after 4:45pm valid next day. Separate fees for Koh Ker, Kulen Mountain
data.transport_train: N/A (no trains to Siem Reap)
data.transport_bus: Buses from Phnom Penh (6hrs, $10-15). Bangkok to Siem Reap (10hrs)
data.transport_car: Tuk-tuk from Siem Reap town ($15-20/day). Motorbike rental $5-8/day. Taxi $25-40/day
data.visit_duration: 1 day minimum. 3 days ideal for main temples. 7 days for comprehensive visit
data.best_time_to_visit: Nov-Mar (dry, cool 20-30°C). Sunrise 5:30-6am magical. Avoid Apr-May extreme heat. Early morning (5-9am) before crowds
data.detailed_description: Largest religious monument in the world, originally Hindu temple dedicated to Vishnu, later Buddhist. UNESCO World Heritage Site spanning 162 hectares. Built by Khmer King Suryavarman II (1113-1150). Features five lotus-shaped towers representing Mt. Meru (center of universe). Intricate bas-reliefs depict Hindu epics, 1,200m of galleries. Famous for sunrise reflections in lotus pond. Part of vast Angkor Archaeological Park with 90+ temples including Ta Prohm (Tomb Raider), Bayon (216 faces).
data.history: Built early 12th century as state temple and capital city for Khmer Empire. Originally dedicated to Vishnu, gradually transformed to Buddhist temple (14th century). Abandoned in 15th century after Khmer Empire declined. Rediscovered by French naturalist Henri Mouhot in 1860s. Survived Khmer Rouge regime (1975-79). Major restoration ongoing since 1990s. Draws over 2 million visitors annually pre-COVID.
tips: Sunrise at Angkor Wat requires 5am arrival - very crowded at main pond
Alternative sunrise: Pre Rup or Phnom Bakheng (fewer people)
Dress code: shoulders/knees covered for temple entry
Bring water, sunscreen, hat - hot and humid
Hire knowledgeable guide ($20-40/day) for historical context
Avoid midday heat (11am-2pm) - temples close temporarily

data.opening_hours: Main Deck: 9am-10:30pm (last entry 10pm). Top Deck: 9am-10:15pm (last entry 9:30pm). Open daily
data.admission_adult: ¥1,200 Main Deck, ¥2,800 Top Deck (both decks ¥3,000)
data.admission_notes: Online booking cheaper. Combo tickets available. Top Deck advance booking required
data.transport_train: Akabanebashi Station (Oedo Line) - 5 min walk. Kamiyacho Station (Hibiya Line) - 7 min walk
data.transport_bus: Toei Bus to Toranomon Hills stop
data.transport_car: Parking ¥600/hour (150 spaces). Expensive - public transport recommended
data.best_time_to_visit: Sunset (4-6pm) for day/night views. Weekday mornings less crowded. Winter clearest skies. Avoid weekends/holidays
data.detailed_description: Iconic 333m tall lattice tower inspired by Eiffel Tower. Completed 1958 as world's tallest self-supporting steel tower (held record until 2012). Tokyo's symbol before Tokyo Skytree. Two observation decks: Main Deck (150m) with 360° views, Top Deck (250m) with floor-to-ceiling windows. Illuminated nightly - orange in winter, white in summer. Special lighting for events. Clear days: see Mt. Fuji (80km away). Shopping/dining at base (FootTown).
data.history: Built 1957-58 during Japan's post-war economic boom. Required 4,000 tons of steel (partly from US tanks from Korean War). Opened December 23, 1958. Originally for broadcasting antenna. Withstood 2011 Tōhoku earthquake with no damage. Painted international orange/white for aviation safety (repainted every 5 years). Was Japan's tallest structure until Tokyo Skytree (2012). Cultural icon in anime, films, tourism.
tips: Book Top Deck online in advance - timed entry
Visit at sunset for day and night views in one trip
Main Deck sufficient for most - Top Deck expensive
Clear winter days best for Mt. Fuji views
FootTown has One Piece theme park, restaurants, aquarium
Illumination changes color for holidays/events

data.opening_hours: Climbing season: Early Jul - mid Sep. 5th Station viewpoints: year-round. Trails close in winter (dangerous)
data.admission_adult: ¥2,000 voluntary climbing fee (conservation). 5th Station free access
data.admission_notes: Mountain huts ¥6,000-10,000/night. Advance booking essential in season. Gear rental available
data.transport_train: Train to Kawaguchiko or Gotemba, then bus to 5th Station (1.5hrs from Tokyo, ¥4,000 return)
data.transport_bus: Direct highway bus from Tokyo/Shinjuku to 5th Station (2.5hrs, ¥2,800-3,900)
data.transport_car: 2hrs from Tokyo. 5th Station parking ¥1,000. Road may close due to weather
data.visit_duration: Viewing: few hours. Climbing: 2 days (ascent 5-7hrs, descent 3-5hrs)
data.best_time_to_visit: Climbing: Jul-Aug (trails open, mountain huts operate). Viewing: Oct-Feb (clear skies, less clouds). Avoid typhoon season Aug-Sep
data.detailed_description: Japan's tallest mountain (3,776m/12,389ft) and active stratovolcano. Sacred site, pilgrimage destination, UNESCO World Heritage. Perfectly symmetrical cone visible from Tokyo on clear days. Official climbing season Jul-early Sep when snow melts, huts open. Four trails: Yoshida (most popular), Fujinomiya (shortest), Subashiri, Gotemba. Summit crater 500m diameter. Sunrise from summit ('Goraiko') highly prized. 5th Station (halfway) accessible year-round for views without climbing.
data.history: Last erupted 1707-08 (Hōei eruption). Sacred in Shinto religion - home to goddess Konohanasakuya-hime. Climbing prohibited to women until 1872. First foreigner to climb: British diplomat Sir Rutherford Alcock (1860). Became UNESCO World Heritage Site 2013 (cultural site, not natural). Over 300,000 people climb annually. Featured in Hokusai's famous 'Great Wave' woodblock print series.
tips: Climbing outside Jul-Sep extremely dangerous - trails closed
Book mountain huts months ahead for Jul-Aug
Start climb afternoon, sleep at 7/8th station, summit for sunrise
Altitude sickness common - ascend slowly, stay hydrated
Bring warm clothes even summer - summit near freezing
5th Station accessible without climbing - great views, shops, restaurants

data.opening_hours: Skybridge/Observation Deck: Tue-Sun 9am-9pm. Closed Mondays (except public holidays). Last entry 8:30pm
data.admission_notes: Book online in advance (sells out fast). Limited walk-up tickets at 8:30am (200 tickets only)
data.transport_train: LRT/MRT to KLCC station (direct underground connection)
data.transport_bus: Rapid KL bus: Pasar Seni or Sultan Ismail stations
data.transport_car: KLCC parking RM 3/hour weekdays, RM 5/hour weekends. First hour free with RM 50 spend
data.best_time_to_visit: Late afternoon (4-6pm) for day/night views. Weekday mornings less crowded. Book 2-4 weeks ahead for weekends
data.detailed_description: Twin skyscrapers 451.9m tall (88 floors), world's tallest twin towers and tallest buildings 1998-2004. Designed by César Pelli with Islamic geometric patterns. Connected by Skybridge at 41st-42nd floors (170m high) - highest 2-story bridge in world. Observation Deck on 86th floor (370m). Stainless steel and glass façade. Houses Petronas (oil company) offices, Suria KLCC mall (6 floors), Petrosains science center, art gallery. Symbol of Malaysia's modernity.
data.history: Completed 1996-1998. Built on former Selangor Turf Club site. Tower 1 by Japanese contractor Hazama, Tower 2 by Samsung. Broke ground 1993. Held 'world's tallest building' title 1998-2004 (surpassed by Taipei 101). Foundation piles go 120m deep due to soft limestone bedrock. Design incorporates Islamic art motifs - floor plate based on Rub el Hizb (Islamic geometric pattern). Cost $1.6 billion. Featured in 'Entrapment' (1999 film).
tips: Book tickets online 1-4 weeks ahead - sells out fast especially weekends
Limited 200 walk-up tickets at 8:30am (queue early)
Visit includes Skybridge (41st floor) + Observation Deck (86th)
Bring passport/ID - required for ticket collection
Photography allowed but no tripods
Suria KLCC mall below has food courts, shops - combine visits

data.opening_hours: Cruise boats: year-round. Most cruises 2D/1N or 3D/2N. Day trips 8am-5pm
data.admission_adult: Bay entrance fee: 120,000-200,000 VND (~$5-8). Cruises: $100-500 depending on luxury
data.admission_child: Half price entrance (under 10). Cruise prices vary by operator
data.admission_notes: Entrance fee separate from cruise cost. Cave visits extra 100,000 VND. Kayaking often included in cruise
data.transport_train: No direct train. Train to Hanoi, then bus/transfer to Halong Bay
data.transport_bus: From Hanoi: 3.5hrs by bus (150,000-300,000 VND). Most cruises include Hanoi pickup
data.transport_car: From Hanoi: 165km, 3-4hrs drive. Parking at cruise ports
data.visit_duration: 2 days/1 night cruise recommended minimum. 3D/2N for relaxed experience
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May, Sep-Nov (warm, dry, clear). Avoid Jun-Aug (hot, rainy, typhoons). Dec-Feb cool but sometimes foggy
data.detailed_description: UNESCO World Heritage Site with 1,600+ limestone islands and islets rising from emerald waters. Covers 1,553 km² in Gulf of Tonkin. Famous for karst landscape, floating villages, caves (Sung Sot, Thien Cung), and rock formations. Most visitors take overnight cruises with meals, kayaking, cave visits, swimming. Day trips available but rushed. Activities: kayaking, swimming, tai chi on deck, cooking class, squid fishing. Two main departure ports: Tuan Chau (larger ships) and Got (smaller authentic boats).
data.history: Name means 'Descending Dragon' from legend of dragon family creating islands to defend Vietnam. Inhabited for 30,000+ years. Strategic military importance through history. Designated UNESCO Heritage 1994 (natural), expanded 2000 (geological). Became one of New7Wonders of Nature (2011). Cat Ba Island inhabited with national park. Now major tourism industry with 2,000+ cruise boats. Environmental concerns leading to regulations on boat numbers, waste.
tips: Book 2-3 star cruise minimum for decent quality (avoid budget party boats)
Most cruises include Hanoi transfer - check what's included
Bring motion sickness medicine if prone to seasickness
March-May best weather, fewer tourists than Oct-Nov
Sung Sot Cave (Surprise Cave) most impressive cave to visit
Avoid cruises during Vietnamese holidays (Tet) - very crowded

data.opening_hours: 24/7 accessible. Best viewing: 8am-5pm for daylight. Sunrise 5:30am, sunset 6pm
data.admission_adult: Most terraces free or small donation (20,000-50,000 IDR ~$1-3). Jatiluwih UNESCO site: 40,000 IDR (~$2.50)
data.admission_notes: Tegallalang most touristy (swings extra 100,000-200,000 IDR). Jatiluwih more authentic
data.transport_train: N/A (no trains in Bali)
data.transport_bus: Limited public buses. Most visitors rent scooter or hire driver
data.transport_car: Scooter rental 50,000-70,000 IDR/day (~$3-5). Car + driver $50-70/day. Tegallalang: 20min north of Ubud. Jatiluwih: 1.5hrs from Ubud
data.visit_duration: 2-3 hours per site. Full day for multiple terraces
data.best_time_to_visit: Apr-Oct dry season (lush, green, sunny). Avoid Dec-Mar rainy. Early morning (7-9am) or late afternoon (4-6pm) best light, fewer crowds
data.detailed_description: Iconic emerald-green terraced rice paddies carved into hillsides. Most famous: Tegallalang (near Ubud, tourist-friendly, Instagram swings), Jatiluwih (UNESCO Heritage, 600 hectares, most expansive, authentic). Others: Campuhan Ridge, Sidemen Valley. Traditional subak irrigation system (over 1,000 years old) - UNESCO Cultural Landscape. Terraces sculpted for optimal water distribution. Rice planted/harvested by hand using traditional methods. Many terraces have cafes, swings, zip lines. Peak green season after planting (Nov-Mar). Harvest Feb-Apr and Aug-Oct.
data.history: Subak cooperative water management system dates to 9th century. UNESCO World Heritage Cultural Landscape (2012) for Jatiluwih and surrounding temples. Subak system reflects Tri Hita Karana philosophy (harmony between humans, nature, gods). Each subak governed democratically by farmers. Water allocation from mountain temples to sea. Threatened by tourism development and conversion to cash crops. Now protected cultural heritage.
tips: Tegallalang very touristy, crowded, swings expensive - go early (7am)
Jatiluwih more authentic, peaceful, bigger - worth longer drive
Wear good shoes - paths can be slippery and muddy
Bring small bills for donations/parking
Rice greenest Nov-Mar (after planting), turns golden before harvest
Hire scooter for flexibility - cheap and fun

data.opening_hours: Outdoor gardens: 5am-2am daily. Cloud Forest/Flower Dome: 9am-9pm (last entry 8pm). Supertree Observatory: 9am-9pm
data.admission_adult: Outdoor gardens FREE. Cloud Forest + Flower Dome: S$28 (~$21 USD). Supertree: S$14. OCBC Skyway: S$12
data.admission_child: Cloud Forest + Flower Dome: S$15 (ages 3-12). Free under 3
data.admission_notes: Combo tickets available. Book online for discounts. Singapore residents get reduced rates
data.transport_train: MRT: Bayfront Station (CE1/DT16) - 5 min walk via underground link
data.transport_bus: Bus 400 from Marina Bay Sands
data.transport_car: Parking: S$4/hour weekdays, S$5/hour weekends at Gardens by the Bay car parks
data.visit_duration: Half day (3-4 hours). Full day if including all conservatories
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (7-9am) for outdoor gardens (cool, quiet). Evening (7-8pm) for Supertree light show (free, daily 7:45pm and 8:45pm). Avoid weekends
data.detailed_description: 101-hectare nature park in central Singapore featuring futuristic Supertrees (25-50m vertical gardens), Cloud Forest (indoor mountain with waterfall, cool-mist environment), Flower Dome (world's largest glass greenhouse with Mediterranean plants). Free outdoor gardens: OCBC Skyway (22m high walkway between Supertrees), Dragonfly and Kingfisher Lakes, Children's Garden. Nightly Garden Rhapsody light/music show on Supertrees (7:45pm, 8:45pm). Eco-sustainable design with solar panels, rainwater harvesting.
data.history: Opened 2012 as part of Singapore's 'Garden City' vision. Built on reclaimed land next to Marina Reservoir. Designed by Grant Associates (UK) and Wilkinson Eyre Architects. Cost S$1 billion. Supertrees designed as vertical gardens with photovoltaic cells. Cloud Forest recreates 2,000m altitude environment. Won World Building of the Year 2012. Over 50 million visitors since opening. Featured in 'Crazy Rich Asians' (2018), 'Westworld' (2016).
tips: Outdoor gardens and Supertree light show completely FREE
Cloud Forest better than Flower Dome (prioritize if choosing one)
Visit conservatories midday when outdoor gardens too hot
Garden Rhapsody light show: 7:45pm and 8:45pm daily (free, best from ground)
OCBC Skyway sunset timing best for photos
Bring jacket for Cloud Forest (22°C, humid)

data.opening_hours: Daily 10am-11pm (last entry 10:15pm). Extended hours for special events
data.admission_adult: Level 124+125: AED 169 (~$46) off-peak, AED 254 peak. Level 148: AED 399 off-peak, AED 533 peak. Sky: AED 769
data.admission_child: Level 124+125: AED 137 (ages 4-12). Free under 4. Level 148: AED 319
data.admission_notes: Prime hours (4-6:30pm) most expensive. Book online cheaper. Same-day tickets limited and expensive
