data.transport_bus: Bus F13 from various Dubai locations
data.transport_car: Parking at Dubai Mall (AED 20/first 4hrs). Free with AED 250 spend. Valet available
data.best_time_to_visit: Sunset (4-6:30pm) for day/night views but most expensive. Early morning (10-11am) cheapest, clearest. Avoid Fri/Sat peak
data.detailed_description: World's tallest building at 828m (2,717 ft) with 163 floors. Held title since 2010. Designed by Adrian Smith (SOM) inspired by regional desert flower Hymenocallis. Three observation decks: At The Top (124th-125th floors, 452m), At The Top SKY (148th floor, 555m - highest observation deck in world), The Lounge (152-154th floors). Y-shaped floor plan for residential, hotel (Armani), and office space. Features Armani Hotel, luxury residences, corporate suites. Dubai Fountain show at base (free, daily 6pm-11pm).
data.history: Construction 2004-2010 (6 years). Officially opened January 4, 2010. Built by South Korean Samsung C&T. Cost $1.5 billion. Originally called Burj Dubai, renamed for UAE President Khalifa bin Zayed. Designed to be centerpiece of Downtown Dubai. Uses 330,000 m³ of concrete, 39,000 tonnes of steel. Foundation: 192 piles 50m deep. Holds multiple world records: tallest structure, tallest free-standing structure, highest observation deck, elevator with longest travel distance (504m), most floors. Featured in 'Mission Impossible: Ghost Protocol' (Tom Cruise climbed exterior).
tips: Book online 1-2 weeks ahead for best prices and time slots
Off-peak hours (10am-3:30pm, 7pm-close) much cheaper than prime (4-6:30pm)
Level 124+125 sufficient for most - 148 very expensive
Clear winter days (Nov-Mar) best for visibility
Combine with Dubai Fountain show (free, 6-11pm every 30min)
Security check can take 30min - arrive early

data.opening_hours: Summer (Apr-Sep): 6am-6pm. Winter (Oct-Mar): 6am-4pm. Petra by Night: Mon/Wed/Thu 8:30-10:30pm
data.admission_adult: 1-day: 50 JOD (~$70). 2-day: 55 JOD. 3-day: 60 JOD. Petra by Night: 17 JOD extra
data.admission_notes: Jordan Pass (70 JOD) includes Petra 1-day + visa + 40 sites. Best value if staying 3+ days in Jordan
data.transport_train: N/A (no trains to Petra)
data.transport_bus: JETT bus from Amman (3hrs, 10 JOD). Minibus from Wadi Musa town (walking distance from entrance)
data.transport_car: From Amman: 3hrs south via Desert Highway. Parking near visitor center (free)
data.visit_duration: Full day minimum. 2-3 days to see major sites properly
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May, Sep-Nov (20-30°C, comfortable). Avoid Jun-Aug (40°C+). Winter (Dec-Feb) cool but can rain. Early entry (6am) for Treasury without crowds
data.detailed_description: Ancient Nabataean city carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs. UNESCO World Heritage Site and New7Wonders. Famous for The Treasury (Al-Khazneh) - elaborate 40m façade carved 100 AD. Main sites: Siq (1.2km narrow gorge entrance), Street of Facades, Roman Theater (3,000 seats), Royal Tombs, Monastery (Ad-Deir, 800+ steps, larger than Treasury). Originally inhabited 6th century BC, thrived as trade route capital. Abandoned 7th century AD. Rediscovered 1812 by Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt. Over 600 tombs, 800+ monuments carved from rock.
data.history: Established by Nabataeans as capital around 300 BC. Prospered as caravan crossroads for silk, spice routes. Ingenious water management with dams, cisterns. Absorbed by Roman Empire 106 AD. Declined as trade routes shifted, abandoned after earthquakes. Bedouins inhabited ruins until 1980s. UNESCO Heritage 1985. Indiana Jones 'Last Crusade' (1989) filmed Treasury scene. Now Jordan's most valuable tourist attraction. Archaeological work ongoing - estimated 85% remains unexcavated.
tips: Buy Jordan Pass online ($99) - includes Petra ticket, visa, 40+ sites (saves money if 3+ days)
Arrive at 6am opening to see Treasury alone (massive crowds by 9am)
Wear comfortable hiking shoes - 8-10km walking on uneven terrain
Bring 4+ liters water, sun protection - no shade, very hot
Monastery hike (800 steps) worth effort - less crowded, larger than Treasury
Donkey/camel/horse rides available but negotiate price first

data.opening_hours: Daily 7am-5pm (summer until 6pm). Closed major Islamic holidays. Sphinx opens 8am
data.admission_adult: 400 EGP (~$13) pyramids area. Great Pyramid interior: +200 EGP. Khafre interior: +100 EGP. Sphinx free with entry
data.admission_child: Half price (with student ID). Free under 6
data.admission_notes: Sound & Light show extra 450 EGP. Camel rides extra (negotiate 100-200 EGP). Photography permit extra
data.transport_train: Cairo Metro to Giza Station, then taxi/Uber (20min, 50-80 EGP)
data.transport_bus: Bus 355, 357 from Tahrir Square (slow, crowded)
data.transport_car: Taxi/Uber from Cairo (30-45min, 150-250 EGP depending on traffic). Parking available
data.best_time_to_visit: Oct-Apr (cooler, 15-25°C). Summer (May-Sep) extremely hot 35-40°C. Early morning (7-9am) before crowds/heat. Avoid Fridays (prayer day, crowded)
data.detailed_description: Last remaining of Seven Wonders of Ancient World. Three main pyramids: Great Pyramid of Khufu (146.5m, largest, built 2580-2560 BC with 2.3 million stone blocks), Pyramid of Khafre (appears taller, still has smooth limestone cap), Pyramid of Menkaure (smallest). Great Sphinx (73m long, 20m high limestone statue with lion body, human head, possibly Pharaoh Khafre's face). Part of Giza Necropolis. Boats, temples, smaller pyramids for queens, mastaba tombs of nobles. Sound & Light show nightly. New Grand Egyptian Museum opening nearby (world's largest archaeological museum).
data.history: Built during Old Kingdom 4th Dynasty (2580-2510 BC). Great Pyramid originally 146.5m tall (now 138.8m due to erosion), world's tallest structure for 3,800 years. Built as tombs for pharaohs. Exact construction method debated (ramps, levers, etc). Required 100,000+ workers over 20 years. Originally covered in smooth white limestone casing (mostly stripped in Middle Ages for Cairo buildings). Interior passages, chambers discovered/rediscovered through centuries. Napoleon visited 1798. Modern archaeology began 1880s. UNESCO Heritage 1979.
tips: Arrive right at 7am opening - crowds surge by 9am, unbearable heat by 11am
Hire guide (100-200 EGP) to avoid scammers and learn history
Ignore aggressive camel riders/vendors - say 'La shukran' (no thanks) firmly
Great Pyramid interior tickets limited - buy early if interested (claustrophobic, hot, narrow)
Bring water, sun protection - no shade, very hot, dusty
Panorama Point best photo spot for all 3 pyramids (tip expected)

data.opening_hours: Daily 9:30am-3:30pm (last ferry). Extended summer hours. Closed Dec 25. Crown: limited slots
data.admission_adult: $24 ferry + pedestal, $25.50 crown access, $18.50 grounds only (Liberty + Ellis Island)
data.admission_notes: Crown tickets sell out months ahead. Book 3-6 months early. Pedestal book 2-4 weeks ahead. Ferry included in all tickets
data.transport_train: Subway to South Ferry (1), Whitehall (R/W), or Bowling Green (4/5). Then Statue Cruises ferry (30min)
data.transport_bus: M5, M15, M20 to Battery Park. Ferry from Battery Park (NYC) or Liberty State Park (NJ)
data.transport_car: Limited parking at Battery Park ($30-50). Liberty State Park (NJ) easier parking ($7)
data.best_time_to_visit: Weekday mornings (first ferry 9:30am). Avoid summer weekends/holidays. Sep-Nov fewer crowds. Book crown 3-6 months ahead
data.detailed_description: Iconic 93m copper statue (46m figure, 47m pedestal) gifted by France in 1886. Designed by sculptor Frédéric Auguste Bartholdi, metal framework by Gustave Eiffel. Depicts Libertas (Roman goddess of freedom) holding torch and tablet inscribed 'July 4, 1776'. National Monument and UNESCO Heritage Site. Visit includes ferry to Liberty Island, pedestal/museum access. Crown access (advance booking) climbs 377 steps for harbor views. Ellis Island Immigration Museum included in ticket (20min ferry away).
data.history: Gift from France commemorating US independence centennial and Franco-American friendship. Designed 1870s, constructed in France, shipped in 350 pieces, assembled 1884-1886. Dedicated October 28, 1886. Originally copper color, turned green patina from oxidation. Torch replaced 1986 during centennial restoration. Ellis Island processed 12 million immigrants 1892-1954 (now museum). Closed after 9/11, reopened 2004 (pedestal), 2009 (crown). Featured in countless films, symbol of American freedom.
tips: Crown tickets sell out 3-6 months ahead - book ASAP if interested
First ferry (9:30am) least crowded - book that slot
Security screening strict - no large bags, tripods allowed
Crown climb: 377 steps, narrow spiral stairs, not for claustrophobic/mobility issues
Pedestal offers good views without crown climb intensity
Ellis Island included - allocate 2+ hours for museum
facilities: Limited wheelchair access (pedestal via elevator, no crown)

data.opening_hours: Pedestrian/bike access: 5am-6:30pm (winter), 5am-9pm (summer). Driving: 24/7. Visitor plaza: 9am-6pm
data.admission_adult: FREE to walk/bike. Driving: $9.75-$10.25 (southbound only, FasTrak or pay-by-plate)
data.admission_notes: No toll booth - electronic only. Cash not accepted. Rental cars billed later. Southbound (SF-bound) only
data.transport_train: BART to Embarcadero, then bus 30 to toll plaza. Or ferry to Sausalito, walk to bridge
data.transport_bus: Muni bus 28, 29 to toll plaza. Golden Gate Transit 70, 101 cross bridge
data.transport_car: From SF: US-101 North. Parking at Vista Point (north) or Fort Point (south). Both free
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (before 9am) for clear weather, fewer crowds. May-Sep often foggy. Sep-Nov clearest skies. Sunset beautiful but cold/windy
data.detailed_description: Iconic 2.7km suspension bridge spanning Golden Gate strait (SF Bay entrance to Pacific Ocean). Completed 1937, was world's longest/tallest suspension bridge until 1964. International Orange color for visibility in fog. Two 227m towers, main span 1,280m. 6-lane highway, pedestrian sidewalks. Art Deco design by Joseph Strauss (chief engineer), Irving Morrow (architect). Views from Battery Spencer (Marin side) and Crissy Field (SF side). Fog often shrouds bridge - nicknamed 'Karl the Fog'.
data.history: Completed May 27, 1937 after 4 years construction. Deemed impossible due to strong currents, deep water, frequent fog. Cost $35 million ($1.5B today). 11 workers died during construction. Survived 1989 Loma Prieta earthquake with minimal damage. Originally tolls collected both directions, now southbound only. Painted continuously to prevent rust - team starts at one end, when finished starts over. Featured in countless films (Hitchcock's Vertigo, X-Men, Rise of Planet Apes).
tips: Walk across (1.7 miles/2.7km one way) - most popular activity, FREE
East sidewalk (bay side) usually open to pedestrians, west to bikes
Bring layers - bridge cold, windy even when SF sunny
Battery Spencer viewpoint (Marin side) best photo spot
Fog thickest Jun-Aug mornings - bridge may be invisible
Bike rental popular - ride to Sausalito, ferry back ($40-50)
facilities: Free parking (Vista Point north, Fort Point south)

data.opening_hours: 24/7 open (public space). Peak activity: 8am-midnight. New Year's Eve ball drop: Dec 31 6pm-Jan 1 1am
data.admission_adult: Free (public space)
data.admission_notes: Broadway shows $50-300+. Restaurants vary. Museums nearby charge admission
data.transport_train: Subway: Times Sq-42 St (N/Q/R/W/S/1/2/3/7). 42 St-Port Authority (A/C/E). 49 St (N/R/W)
data.transport_bus: M7, M20, M42, M104 buses
data.transport_car: Parking garages $30-60/day. Not recommended - traffic terrible, expensive
data.visit_duration: 1-2 hours (just to see). Half day if including Broadway show
data.best_time_to_visit: Evening (7-10pm) when all lights/billboards fully lit. New Year's Eve iconic but extremely crowded (arrive 12+ hours early). Avoid weekday rush hours
data.detailed_description: Major commercial intersection in Midtown Manhattan, famous for massive electronic billboards, Broadway theaters, neon lights. Nicknamed 'Crossroads of the World' and 'The Great White Way'. Covers Broadway and 7th Avenue intersection, 42nd-47th Streets. Over 50 million visitors annually. New Year's Eve ball drop tradition since 1907 draws 1+ million. TKTS booth sells discounted same-day Broadway tickets. Red steps (Father Duffy Square) popular sitting area. Pedestrian plazas installed 2009 (previously vehicle traffic).
data.history: Originally Longacre Square, renamed 1904 when NY Times moved headquarters there. Became theater district in early 1900s. Notorious for crime/decay 1960s-80s, revitalized 1990s with Disney investment. First electric billboard 1904. Ball drop tradition began 1907 (originally iron/wood, now LED crystal). Pedestrianized 2009 to reduce traffic, increase foot traffic. Now zoned to require illuminated signs - intentionally maintained as commercial spectacle. Featured in countless films, TV shows (NYPD, TGIF).
tips: FREE to visit - just walk around, take photos
Evening (after 7pm) best for full lighting effect
Avoid costumed characters - aggressive tipping, sometimes scams
TKTS booth: discounted Broadway tickets (day-of, 20-50% off)
New Year's Eve requires arriving 12+ hours early, no bathrooms, very cold
Red steps good free seating/people-watching spot

data.opening_hours: Open year-round. Roads: Most close Nov-Apr (winter). North Entrance (Gardiner) open year-round. West Entrance opens mid-Apr
data.admission_adult: $35 per vehicle (7-day pass), $30 motorcycle, $20 per person (bike/walk-in)
data.admission_notes: Annual Pass $70. America the Beautiful Pass $80 (all national parks). Valid 7 days
data.transport_train: No trains to park. Nearest: Amtrak to Salt Lake City (390mi) or Denver (560mi)
data.transport_bus: Limited shuttles from gateway towns. Car rental essential
data.transport_car: From Denver: 560mi, 8-9hrs. Bozeman, MT: 90mi, 2hrs (closest airport). 5 entrances: North, Northeast, East, South, West
data.visit_duration: 3-5 days minimum for highlights. 7+ days for comprehensive visit
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Jun (spring, baby animals, fewer crowds) or Sep (fall colors, elk rut, less crowded). Jul-Aug peak (crowded, all facilities open). Dec-Mar winter sports but limited access
data.detailed_description: First national park in world (1872), covering 2.2 million acres across Wyoming, Montana, Idaho. Sits atop massive supervolcano with 10,000+ geothermal features - half of world's geysers. Famous for Old Faithful geyser (erupts every 90min), Grand Prismatic Spring (largest US hot spring, rainbow colors), Yellowstone Lake (largest high-altitude lake in North America), Grand Canyon of Yellowstone (yellow cliffs, waterfalls). Wildlife: grizzly/black bears, wolves, bison, elk, moose. UNESCO World Heritage Site.
data.history: Protected March 1, 1872 as world's first national park (by President Grant). Native Americans inhabited for 11,000 years. Fur trappers explored 1800s with tales of geysers dismissed as myths. Ferdinand Hayden led 1871 expedition that led to park establishment. US Army managed 1886-1918, then National Park Service. Major wildfires 1988 burned 36% of park - allowed to burn naturally, led to ecological debate. Supervolcano last erupted 640,000 years ago - scientists monitor seismic activity. 4 million visitors annually.
tips: Reserve lodging 6-12 months ahead for summer (books out fast)
Grand Loop Road: 142-mile circuit hits major sites (allow 2 days minimum)
Old Faithful erupts every 60-110min - check prediction +/-10min
Stay 300 yards from bears, 25 yards from bison/elk (dangerous!)
Bring bear spray ($40-50) if hiking backcountry
Gas up before entering - stations limited, expensive inside park

data.opening_hours: Falls: 24/7 viewable. Attractions: mostly 9am-8pm (summer), reduced winter hours. Journey Behind Falls: 9am-5pm winter, 9am-8pm summer
data.admission_adult: Falls viewing FREE. Journey Behind Falls: CAD $16.50. Maid of Mist boat: CAD $30. Niagara Parks Power Station: CAD $23
data.admission_child: Journey Behind Falls: CAD $10.50 (6-12). Maid of Mist: CAD $18.50. Free under 5
data.admission_notes: Niagara Falls Adventure Pass bundles attractions (CAD $72 adult). Some attractions seasonal (May-Oct)
data.transport_train: VIA Rail/Amtrak to Niagara Falls station (Canada or US side). Then taxi/bus to falls
data.transport_bus: WEGO bus system serves attractions (CAD $9 all-day pass). Megabus/Greyhound from Toronto (1.5hrs)
data.transport_car: From Toronto: 130km, 1.5hrs via QEW. Parking CAD $10-25 near falls. Border crossing if coming from US side
data.visit_duration: Full day (both sides). Overnight to include evening illumination
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Sep (all attractions open, weather nice). Jun-Aug peak crowds. Winter sees frozen falls (stunning) but limited activities. Evening illumination year-round
data.detailed_description: Three waterfalls on US-Canada border: Horseshoe Falls (Canadian, 57m drop, 790m wide - largest), American Falls (56m drop, 260m wide), Bridal Veil Falls (smallest). Combined flow rate 2,832 m³/second - North America's most powerful. Niagara River drains Lake Erie to Lake Ontario. Best views from Canadian side (Table Rock). Popular attractions: Maid of Mist boat tour (gets you soaked at base), Journey Behind Falls (tunnels behind cascade), Skylon Tower (360° observation), Niagara Parks Power Station (new attraction). Evening LED illumination year-round. Winter: partially frozen falls stunning but cold.
data.history: Formed ~12,000 years ago after Ice Age glaciers retreated. Native Americans called it 'Thundering Water'. First European to see: Father Louis Hennepin (1677). Erosion causes falls to move upstream ~1m per year. Hydroelectric power generation began 1881 - now provides power to Ontario/New York. Daredevils attempted barrel rides since 1901 (many died, now illegal). Niagara Falls, Ontario (Canadian city) developed as honeymoon destination in 1800s. Clifton Hill area now tourist trap with museums, casinos, Skylon Tower.
tips: Canadian side has better views - stay there if choosing one side
Maid of Mist boat tour essential - operates May-Oct (worth getting wet!)
Bring poncho or buy one ($2-5) - you WILL get soaked
Evening illumination free (daily 5-11pm) - stunning rainbow colors
Winter visit: frozen falls beautiful but dress very warm
Clifton Hill touristy but fun if you like kitsch (wax museum, haunted houses)

data.opening_hours: Daily 6am-5:30pm. Entry slots: 6am, 7am, 8am, 9am, 10am, 11am, 12pm, 1pm, 2pm. Must exit within 4hrs of entry time
data.admission_adult: 152 PEN (~$40) Machu Picchu only. 200 PEN (~$53) with Huayna Picchu mountain. 200 PEN with Machu Picchu Mountain
data.admission_notes: MUST book online weeks/months ahead (sells out). Passport required. Entry time slot strictly enforced. No re-entry
data.transport_train: Train from Cusco to Aguas Calientes (3.5-4hrs, $70-100+ USD each way). Peru Rail or Inca Rail. Then bus or hike up
data.transport_bus: Bus from Aguas Calientes to Machu Picchu entrance (25min, $12 USD each way). Departs every 15min from 5:30am
data.transport_car: No road access to Machu Picchu. Must take train to Aguas Calientes
data.visit_duration: 4 hours (max allowed in citadel). Full day with travel from Cusco
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Sep dry season (clear, no rain). Jun-Aug peak crowds. Apr and Oct shoulder season (fewer tourists, occasional rain). Arrive on first 6am slot for photos without crowds
data.detailed_description: 15th-century Inca citadel perched 2,430m (7,970ft) in Andes mountains. UNESCO World Heritage and New7Wonders of the World. Built ~1450 under Inca emperor Pachacuti, abandoned during Spanish conquest. Remained unknown to outside world until Hiram Bingham's 1911 'rediscovery'. Terraced stone structures blend seamlessly with mountain. Complex includes temples, palaces, agricultural terraces, 150+ buildings. Intihuatana stone (ritual stone), Temple of Sun, Room of Three Windows. Optional hikes: Huayna Picchu (steep 1hr climb, limited 200 people/day), Machu Picchu Mountain (3hr, less steep).
data.history: Built mid-1400s, likely as estate for Emperor Pachacuti. Abandoned ~1572 during Spanish conquest (Spaniards never found it). Local people knew of site but kept secret. American historian Hiram Bingham brought international attention 1911 (though not technically 'discovered'). Excavations ongoing. Purpose debated - royal estate, religious site, astronomical observatory. Ingenious stone construction without mortar - earthquake resistant. UNESCO Heritage 1983. Now Peru's most visited site (~1.5 million/year). New access restrictions to protect site.
tips: Book tickets 2-6 months ahead (sells out, especially Huayna Picchu)
Acclimatize to altitude in Cusco 2-3 days before visiting
6am first slot best for empty photos (arrive at entrance 5:30am)
Huayna Picchu only 200 tickets/day - book months ahead, very steep
No large bags allowed - lockers in Aguas Calientes
Bring passport - checked at entry, required for ticket
facilities: No food/water allowed inside (some water ok)

data.opening_hours: Daily 8am-7pm. Extended to 8pm in summer (Dec-Feb). Closed during severe weather (lightning)
data.admission_adult: R$89 (~$18 USD) van/train ticket + entry. R$109 for official tour
data.admission_child: R$56 (ages 5-11, Brazilian residents cheaper). Free under 5
data.admission_notes: Price includes Corcovado train OR van. Train more scenic, popular. Book online to avoid lines. Weather can close statue
data.transport_train: Corcovado Rack Railway from Cosme Velho (20min scenic ride, R$89 round-trip)
data.transport_bus: Official vans from Largo do Machado, Copacabana, Barra (R$89 includes entry)
data.transport_car: No private vehicle access to summit. Must use train/van. Park at Cosme Velho station
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (8-9am) for clearest views before clouds. Weekdays less crowded. Apr-Jun, Aug-Oct drier. Avoid Dec-Mar rainy season
data.detailed_description: Iconic 30m (98ft) tall Art Deco statue of Jesus Christ atop 710m Corcovado mountain in Tijuca Forest National Park. Arms span 28m. Built 1922-1931, made of reinforced concrete and soapstone tiles. Overlooks Rio with panoramic views of Sugarloaf Mountain, Copacabana/Ipanema beaches, Guanabara Bay, Maracanã stadium. One of New7Wonders of the World. At night, statue illuminated. Reached by cog train (scenic 20min ride through Atlantic Forest) or van. Access via elevator/escalator from parking area, then 220 steps (or escalators) to base.
data.history: Idea proposed 1859, shelved until 1920. Designed by Brazilian engineer Heitor da Silva Costa, sculpted by French-Polish Paul Landowski, built 1922-1931. Opened October 12, 1931 (Brazil's patron saint day). Funded by Brazilian Catholic community donations. Original lighting by Marconi (radio inventor) from Italy. Struck by lightning multiple times (repaired). Renovated 1980, 2000, 2010. Became UNESCO World Heritage Site 2012 (as part of Rio landscape). Pope visits blessed site. Featured in countless films (Fast Five, 2012, etc). Symbol of Brazilian Christianity and Rio.
tips: Book online 1-2 weeks ahead to avoid sold-out disappointment
Go early (8-9am) before clouds roll in - views clearer
Train more scenic than van but both reach same place
Closed during thunderstorms (lightning risk) - check weather
Bring jacket - windy and cooler at top
Escalators available if you can't climb 220 steps

data.opening_hours: Argentina side: 8am-6pm daily. Brazil side: 9am-5pm. Extended summer hours (Dec-Feb)
data.admission_adult: Argentina: 8,000 ARS (~$8). Brazil: 95 BRL (~$19). Both needed for full experience
data.admission_child: Argentina: 2,000 ARS (6-12). Brazil: 16.50 BRL (2-11). Free younger
data.admission_notes: Multi-day tickets available (Argentina). Brazil ticket valid 2 consecutive days with stamp
data.transport_train: N/A (no trains)
data.transport_bus: Buses from Puerto Iguazú (Argentina) or Foz do Iguaçu (Brazil) to park entrances (30min, ~$2-3)
data.transport_car: From Buenos Aires: 1,350km, 18hrs. Puerto Iguazú: 17km to park. Parking at both entrances
data.visit_duration: Full day each side (2 days ideal). Argentina side has more trails, Brazil better panoramic views
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May, Aug-Nov (moderate temps 20-28°C, less rain). Jun-Jul cooler. Dec-Feb hot/rainy but highest water flow. Avoid Easter/July holidays (crowds)
data.detailed_description: Massive waterfall system on Argentina-Brazil border - largest in world by total flow. 275 individual falls spanning 2.7km. Highest drop: Devil's Throat (Garganta del Diablo) - 82m into horseshoe chasm. Argentina side: walkways through falls, boat rides, jungle trails (most time needed). Brazil side: panoramic boardwalk with full views (2-3hrs sufficient). Average flow 1,746 m³/s. Surrounding subtropical rainforest (UNESCO World Heritage). Wildlife: coatis, toucans, monkeys, butterflies. Both countries have national parks (Argentina 677 km², Brazil 185 km²).
data.history: Formed ~200,000 years ago from volcanic eruptions. Discovered by Spanish conquistador Alvar Núñez Cabeza de Vaca (1541). Name from Guaraní 'y' (water) + 'guasu' (big). Argentina National Park created 1934, Brazil 1939. UNESCO Heritage: Argentina 1984, Brazil 1986. Eleanor Roosevelt visiting exclaimed 'Poor Niagara!' comparing size. Featured in films (The Mission, Indiana Jones 4, Black Panther). Both countries developed extensive infrastructure 1980s-90s. Now 1.5+ million visitors annually (combined).
tips: Visit BOTH sides - Argentina for up-close, Brazil for panoramic (worth it!)
Argentina side needs full day, Brazil side 2-3 hours
Devil's Throat (Argentina) best in morning (light, before mist)
Bring waterproof bag/case - you WILL get soaked on trails
Great Aventura boat tour (Argentina) drives into falls - thrilling, soaking
Wildlife abundant - keep food secured (coatis steal!)

data.opening_hours: Daily 8am-4:30pm (last entry 4pm). Closed Dec 25
data.admission_adult: 614 MXN (~$35 USD): 533 federal + 81 state fee
data.admission_notes: Pay federal + state fees separately (two windows). Credit cards accepted. Sundays free for Mexican nationals/residents
data.transport_train: N/A (no trains to Chichen Itza)
data.transport_bus: ADO bus from Cancún (2.5-3hrs, 200-300 MXN), Merida (1.5hrs, 150 MXN), Playa del Carmen (3hrs). Drops at town, taxi/colectivo to ruins
data.transport_car: From Cancún: 200km, 2.5hrs via highway 180D (toll road, safer, faster). Parking 80 MXN
data.best_time_to_visit: Arrive at 8am opening (fewer crowds, cooler). Avoid 10am-2pm tour bus rush. Mar 20-21 (spring equinox) shadow serpent on pyramid (crowded). Nov-Apr dry season best weather
data.detailed_description: UNESCO World Heritage and New7Wonders Mayan archaeological site. Main pyramid: El Castillo (Temple of Kukulkan) - 24m tall stepped pyramid with 365 steps (one per day), built ~1050-1300 AD. Famous for equinox phenomenon: shadow creates serpent descending pyramid. Other structures: Temple of Warriors (columns topped with carved figures), Great Ball Court (largest in Mesoamerica, 168m long), Sacred Cenote (sacrificial well, 60m diameter), El Caracol (astronomical observatory). No climbing allowed since 2006. Sound & light show evenings (extra fee).
data.history: Major Mayan city 600-1200 AD, political/economic/religious center. Name means 'at the mouth of the well of Itza'. Built in two phases: earlier Mayan (600-900), later Toltec influence (900-1200). Abandoned ~1200-1250 (reasons debated - drought, war). Spanish conquistadors arrived 1500s. Explored/excavated extensively 1840s onwards. Carnegie Institution restored many buildings 1920s-40s. UNESCO Heritage 1988. Named New7Wonders 2007. Climbing banned after tourist fell to death 2006. Now Mexico's 2nd most visited archaeological site (2.6 million/year).
tips: Arrive right at 8am opening to beat tour bus crowds (surge 10am-2pm)
Bring hat, sunscreen, water - extremely hot, no shade
Hire guide at entrance (~500 MXN/1hr) for historical context
No climbing El Castillo pyramid (banned since 2006)
Vendors aggressive - polite 'no gracias' and keep walking
Swim in nearby Cenote Ik Kil after visit (10min drive, refreshing)

data.opening_hours: Daily 5:30am-2am. Main concourse always open. Shops/restaurants vary (typically 7am-9pm)
data.admission_adult: Free (working train station, public building)
data.admission_notes: Guided tours available ($30-35 adults). Free self-guided via audio app. Dining/shopping separate costs
data.transport_train: Metro-North trains, NYC Subway 4/5/6/7/S lines - it's the station itself!
data.transport_bus: M1, M2, M3, M4, M42, M101, M102, M103 buses nearby
data.transport_car: Parking garages nearby ($30-50/day). Not recommended - terminal exists for trains
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (6-8am) when light beams through windows. Midday (noon) when zodiac ceiling most visible. Avoid evening rush hour (5-7pm)
data.detailed_description: Beaux-Arts railway terminal opened 1913, world's largest (44 platforms, 67 tracks). Famous for Main Concourse with 38m high vaulted ceiling featuring painted zodiac constellations (reversed). Four-faced opal clock atop info booth (valued $10-20M). Iconic Tiffany glass clock on 42nd St facade. Whisper Gallery - stand at diagonal corners, whisper heard clearly across (architectural acoustics). Serves Metro-North commuter trains. Lower level: food court (400+ seats), Oyster Bar restaurant (since 1913). Apple Store, shops, Campbell Bar (hidden speakeasy-style bar). National Historic Landmark.
data.history: Opened February 2, 1913 (replaced older 1871 station). Designed by Reed & Stern, Warren & Wetmore. Built by Cornelius Vanderbilt's railroad companies. Ceiling's zodiac backward (from God's perspective vs Earth's - debated if intentional). Nearly demolished 1960s for skyscraper - Jacqueline Kennedy led preservation campaign. Landmark status 1967. Major restoration 1996-1998 ($196M) cleaned ceiling, revealed hidden windows, added retail. Oyster Bar explosion 1997 during renovation (no casualties). Featured in countless films (Superman, Avengers, Gossip Girl, Mad Men).
tips: Completely FREE to explore - it's a working train station
Main Concourse ceiling zodiac constellations worth seeing (look up!)
Whisper Gallery acoustic trick: diagonal corners of arches outside Oyster Bar
Free official app has self-guided audio tour
Oyster Bar restaurant iconic (pricey) - raw bar at counter more affordable
Campbell Bar hidden gem - converted office of 1920s exec (reservations recommended)

data.opening_hours: Cable car: 8am-6pm (winter), 8am-8pm (summer). Weather dependent - can close due to high winds. Check daily
data.admission_adult: R 395 (~$22 USD) cable car round-trip. R 210 one-way
data.admission_notes: Book online for 15% discount. Queue can be 1-2hrs in peak season. Hiking free but strenuous
data.transport_train: No direct train. Metrorail to city, then taxi/bus
data.transport_bus: MyCiTi bus to Kloof Nek (then 15min walk). Hop-on-Hop-off buses to lower cable station
data.transport_car: From city center: 15min drive to lower cable station. Limited parking (arrive early). Uber/taxi recommended
data.visit_duration: 2-3 hours at summit. Half day with travel
data.best_time_to_visit: Oct-Mar (summer, warm, long hours). Early morning (8-10am) clearest, fewer crowds. Avoid Dec-Jan peak season queues. Check wind forecast
data.detailed_description: Iconic flat-topped mountain dominating Cape Town skyline at 1,085m elevation. Part of Table Mountain National Park, UNESCO World Heritage Site. Rotating cable car (360° views during ascent) takes 5min to summit. Summit plateau 3km long with multiple viewpoints, walking trails, dassies (rock hyrax). Panoramic views of Cape Town, Robben Island, Atlantic Ocean, Twelve Apostles peaks. Often covered by 'tablecloth' cloud. Can hike up via Platteklip Gorge (2-3hrs, steep) or other routes. Sunset from summit spectacular but last cable car down is strict.
data.history: Named by Antonio de Saldanha (Portuguese explorer, 1503) - called 'Table of the Cape'. Khoisan people considered it sacred. First recorded ascent 1503. Cable car opened 1929 (rebuilt 1997 with rotating cars). Flora: over 1,470 plant species (more than entire UK) - part of Cape Floral Kingdom. Table Mountain Aerial Cableway Company operates cable car. Rock climbing routes established 1900s. New7Wonders of Nature finalist 2011. Weather station on summit. 'Tablecloth' cloud caused by southeaster wind.
tips: Book cable car online for 15% discount + skip queue
Check wind conditions - cable car closes frequently (refunds given)
Go early morning (8am) for clearest views, fewer people
Bring jacket - summit 5-10°C cooler, very windy
Sunset timing tricky - last cable car down strictly enforced
Hiking up free but strenuous (2-3hrs) - can take cable car down
facilities: Limited wheelchair access (cable car accessible, summit uneven)

data.opening_hours: Park: 6am-6pm daily (both countries). Best viewing hours: early morning 6-9am for light/rainbows
data.admission_adult: Zimbabwe: $30 USD. Zambia: $20 USD. Both needed for full views
data.admission_notes: Multi-entry passes available. Some activities extra: Devil's Pool $155, helicopter flights $160-300
data.transport_train: Rovos Rail luxury train from Pretoria/Cape Town (multi-day, expensive)
data.transport_bus: Long-distance buses to Victoria Falls town (Zimbabwe) or Livingstone (Zambia) from regional cities
data.transport_car: From Johannesburg: 1,600km, 18hrs (not recommended). Livingstone/Vic Falls towns: 10-15min to falls. Taxis readily available
data.visit_duration: Full day (both sides). 2-3 days for activities (rafting, bungee, helicopter)
data.best_time_to_visit: Feb-May high water (most powerful, huge spray). Aug-Dec low water (better views, Devil's Pool accessible Sep-Dec). Apr-May peak flow. Avoid rainy season Nov-Mar for activities
data.detailed_description: World's largest waterfall by total area (1,708m wide, 108m drop). Called 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' (Smoke that Thunders) locally. On Zambezi River at Zambia-Zimbabwe border. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Peak flow 500+ million liters/min. Spray visible 30km away. Zimbabwe side: better overall views, 16 viewpoints along 1.7km trail. Zambia side: closer to main Eastern Cataract, Devil's Pool (natural infinity pool on cliff edge, Sep-Dec only). Activities: bungee jumping (111m), white-water rafting (class V), helicopter/microlight flights ('Flight of Angels'), sunset cruises.
data.history: Named by David Livingstone (1855) after Queen Victoria - first European to see falls. Local Kololo/Lozi people knew as Mosi-oa-Tunya for centuries. Formed ~2 million years ago by erosion. Bridge connecting countries built 1905 during British colonization. Zambian side developed later than Zimbabwe. Tourism grew 1950s-60s. Political instability 1970s-90s reduced visitors. Now major African tourist destination (1+ million/year). Devil's Pool became famous via social media 2010s.
tips: Visit BOTH countries - Zimbabwe for panoramic views, Zambia for up-close
Feb-May high water: most powerful but spray obscures views (bring poncho!)
Sep-Dec low water: better visibility, can access Devil's Pool
Devil's Pool ($155, Sep-Dec only): natural infinity pool on cliff edge - thrilling
Helicopter 'Flight of Angels' ($160-300) worth splurge for aerial views
Zimbabwe side better developed, more viewpoints. Zambia more adventurous
facilities: Hotels in Victoria Falls (Zim) and Livingstone (Zambia)

data.opening_hours: Park: 6am-6pm entry gates. Inside 24/7 if staying overnight. Game drives: early morning 6-9am, late afternoon 3-6pm best
data.admission_adult: $70 per person per day (24hrs). Concession fee $50/vehicle/entry. Camping $30-50/night
data.admission_notes: Fees paid at gate (cash USD or card). Multi-day safari packages include fees. Separate fees for Ngorongoro if combined
data.transport_train: No trains to Serengeti. Nearest: Dar es Salaam (fly to Arusha, then drive)
data.transport_bus: Limited public transport. Most visitors use safari operators from Arusha (6-7hrs drive) or fly to airstrips
data.transport_car: From Arusha: 335km, 7-8hrs via rough roads. 4WD essential. Most use safari operator vehicles
data.visit_duration: 3-5 days minimum. 7+ days for comprehensive safari including Ngorongoro/Tarangire
data.best_time_to_visit: Jul-Oct (dry season, Great Migration river crossings, best wildlife viewing). Dec-Mar (calving season in southern Serengeti, green landscape). Avoid Apr-May (heavy rains, muddy roads)
data.detailed_description: Tanzania's oldest/most famous national park (14,750 km²), UNESCO World Heritage Site. Name from Maasai 'Siringet' (endless plains). Famous for Great Migration: 1.5 million wildebeest, 200,000 zebras, gazelles annually migrate following rains. Dramatic Mara River crossings (Jul-Oct) with crocodile predation. Big Five: lions, leopards, elephants, buffalo, rhinos (rare). Diverse ecosystems: savanna grasslands, acacia woodlands, riverine forests, kopjes (rocky outcrops). Ngorongoro Conservation Area adjacent. Multiple camps/lodges from budget to luxury ($150-1,500/night).
data.history: Established 1951 (originally 1929 as partial game reserve). Maasai people inhabited for centuries, partially relocated when park expanded. Name means 'endless plains' in Maasai. Bernhard Grzimek's 1959 film 'Serengeti Shall Not Die' raised awareness, helped protect park. UNESCO Heritage 1981. Poaching crisis 1970s-80s reduced wildlife, anti-poaching efforts restored populations. Great Migration studied extensively since 1950s. Hot air balloon safaris introduced 1980s. Now Tanzania's most visited park (350,000/year).
tips: Book safari 3-6 months ahead for Jul-Oct peak migration season
3-day minimum recommended - park is huge (14,750 km²)
Great Migration timing: Jun-Jul north, Jul-Oct river crossings, Dec-Mar south calving
Early morning (6-9am) and late afternoon (3-6pm) game drives best animal activity
Mid-range lodges offer better value than luxury ($200-400/night vs $800+)
Combine with Ngorongoro Crater (3hr drive) for complete experience

data.opening_hours: Medina: 24/7 accessible (public space). Souks: typically 9am-8pm. Jemaa el-Fnaa square busiest sunset-midnight. Monuments vary (9am-5pm typical)
data.admission_adult: Medina free to wander. Specific sites: Bahia Palace 70 MAD (~$7), Saadian Tombs 70 MAD, Ben Youssef Madrasa 50 MAD
data.admission_child: Free or half price at monuments
data.admission_notes: Many sites cash only (Moroccan Dirham). Bargaining expected in souks (start 50% of asking price)
data.transport_train: Train from Casablanca (3hrs, 100-150 MAD). Marrakech station 3km from medina, taxi 30-50 MAD
data.transport_bus: CTM/Supratours buses from major cities. Station near medina. Local buses 4-7 MAD
data.transport_car: Riads (hotels) in medina accessible only by foot - park outside medina walls. Parking 20-50 MAD/day
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May, Sep-Nov (20-30°C, pleasant). Avoid Jun-Aug (40°C+ extreme heat). Dec-Feb cool nights but pleasant days. Ramadan: some restaurants closed during day
data.detailed_description: UNESCO World Heritage old city (medina) founded 1070-72. Maze of narrow alleys, souks (markets), riads (traditional houses with courtyards), mosques, palaces. Heart: Jemaa el-Fnaa square - UNESCO Masterpiece of Oral Heritage, filled with snake charmers, storytellers, musicians, food stalls. Souks sell spices, textiles, carpets, leather, metalwork, ceramics. Must-see: Bahia Palace (19th-century palace), Saadian Tombs (16th-century royal necropolis), Koutoubia Mosque (12th-century minaret, 77m tall, non-Muslim exterior only), Ben Youssef Madrasa (Islamic college). Red-brown pisé (rammed earth) buildings. Majorelle Garden (Yves Saint Laurent's villa) outside medina.
data.history: Founded 1070 by Almoravid dynasty as imperial capital. Name means 'Land of God' in Berber. Koutoubia Mosque built 1150s. Saadian dynasty added tombs, palaces 1500s. Declined under French protectorate (1912-56), Rabat became capital. Medina walls (19km circumference) built 1126-27. Jemaa el-Fnaa square has been gathering place for 1,000+ years. UNESCO Heritage 1985. Tourism boomed 1990s-2000s with riad restoration boom. YSL's Majorelle Garden restored 1980. Now Morocco's top tourist destination.
tips: Get 'lost' in medina - part of experience, locals help if needed
Bargain in souks - start 40-50% of asking price, walk away if too high
Jemaa el-Fnaa: sunset onwards most lively, food stalls set up evening
Hire guide first day (200-300 MAD) for orientation, avoid scams
Dress modestly (shoulders/knees covered) - respect local culture
Avoid aggressive 'helpers' - polite 'La shukran' (no thanks)
facilities: Restaurants (from street food to fine dining)

data.opening_hours: Exterior: 24/7 viewable. Guided tours: 9am-5pm daily. Performance schedules vary. Forecourt/bars open late
data.admission_adult: Exterior FREE. Guided tour $43 AUD (~$29 USD). Performances $50-250+ depending on show
data.admission_child: Tour: $23 (ages 5-15). Free under 5. Family packages available
data.admission_notes: Book tours online. Performance tickets separate (Opera Australia, Sydney Symphony, etc). Dining reservations recommended
data.transport_train: Circular Quay Station (5 min walk) - most convenient. Town Hall or St James also walkable
data.transport_bus: Many routes to Circular Quay. Bus 333 from Bondi
data.transport_car: Parking expensive ($40-60/day). Opera Quay car park closest. Public transport recommended
data.best_time_to_visit: Early morning (6-8am) for photos with no crowds. Sunset (5-7pm) for golden light on shells. Tour midday fine. Book performances months ahead
data.detailed_description: Iconic performing arts venue with distinctive white shell-like roof 'sails'. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Designed by Danish architect Jørn Utzon, completed 1973 after 14-year construction. Contains 6 performance spaces: Concert Hall (2,679 seats - largest), Joan Sutherland Theatre (opera/ballet, 1,507 seats), Drama Theatre, Playhouse, Studio, Utzon Room. 1,056 pre-cast concrete roof segments covered in 1,056,006 Swedish-made ceramic tiles. Sits on Bennelong Point in Sydney Harbour. Hosts 1,500+ performances annually (opera, ballet, theatre, symphony). Restaurants: Bennelong (fine dining), Opera Bar (casual, harbour views).
data.history: Design competition won by Jørn Utzon 1957 (233 entries). Construction began 1959, original budget $7M AUD. Utzon resigned 1966 amid conflicts, never saw completed building. Final cost $102M (funded by lottery). Opened October 20, 1973 by Queen Elizabeth II. UNESCO Heritage 2007 (one of youngest sites). Utzon received Pritzker Prize 2003 but never returned to Australia. Renovation/upgrades 2000s-2010s. Celebrates 50th anniversary 2023. Featured in films (Finding Nemo, Mission Impossible 2, X-Men Origins).
tips: Exterior viewing completely FREE - walk around, take photos
Guided tour ($43) worthwhile for interior access and history
Best photos from Mrs Macquarie's Chair (15min walk) for full harbour view
Opera Bar perfect for drinks with harbour view (no booking needed)
Performance tickets: book months ahead for popular shows
Free entry to foyer/bars - can explore without tour/performance

data.opening_hours: Reef accessible year-round. Day trips typically 8am-5pm. Liveaboard cruises 2-7 days. Weather dependent
data.admission_adult: Day trips $150-300 AUD (~$100-200 USD). Liveaboards $500-2,000+. Environmental Management Charge $7/day
data.admission_notes: Prices include boat, snorkeling/diving gear, lunch. Scuba diving certification extra $100-150/dive
data.transport_train: Train to Cairns (from Brisbane 24hrs, expensive). Fly recommended
data.transport_bus: Long-distance buses to Cairns/Port Douglas/Airlie Beach (departure points). Greyhound from Brisbane 30hrs
data.transport_car: From Brisbane: 1,700km, 20+ hrs to Cairns (not practical). Most visitors fly to Cairns/Whitsundays
data.visit_duration: Day trip minimum. 2-3 day liveaboard recommended for remote reefs
data.best_time_to_visit: Jun-Oct (dry season, best visibility 20-30m, calm seas, stinger-free). Apr-May, Nov also good. Avoid Dec-Mar (wet season, box jellyfish, cyclones)
data.detailed_description: World's largest coral reef system - 2,300km long, covering 344,400 km². UNESCO World Heritage Site. Visible from space. Contains 2,900+ individual reefs, 900 islands. Home to 1,500+ fish species, 400+ coral types, 30+ whale/dolphin species, 6 sea turtle species. Main access points: Cairns (closest major city), Port Douglas (upmarket), Airlie Beach (Whitsundays), Townsville. Activities: snorkeling, scuba diving, glass-bottom boats, scenic flights, island stays. Outer reefs healthier than inner reefs. Notable sites: Ribbon Reefs, Cod Hole, Osprey Reef.
data.history: Formed over 20 million years, current structure 6,000-8,000 years old. First European sighting: Captain Cook ran aground 1770 (near Cooktown). Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander peoples used reefs for 60,000+ years. Scientific surveys began 1890s. UNESCO Heritage 1981. Suffered coral bleaching events 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020 due to warming oceans. Protected by Great Barrier Reef Marine Park (established 1975). Now facing climate change threats. Tourism generates $6B AUD annually. Snorkeling/diving tourism began 1980s.
tips: Outer reefs (Agincourt, Ribbon) healthier coral - worth longer boat ride
Jun-Oct best: calm seas, clear water, no stingers (jellyfish)
Cairns most convenient base - most tours, budget friendly
Port Douglas more upscale, less crowded, closer to outer reefs
Liveaboard cruises access remote pristine reefs - worth splurge
Bring reef-safe sunscreen (regular sunscreen damages coral)

data.opening_hours: Park: 30min before sunrise to sunset daily. Varies by season: 5am-7:30pm (summer), 6:30am-7:30pm (winter)
data.admission_adult: $38 AUD (~$25 USD) per person, 3-day park pass
data.admission_notes: 3-day pass valid Uluru and Kata Tjuta. Annual pass $50. Pay at entry gate or online. Climbing banned since 2019
data.transport_train: No trains to Uluru. Nearest: Adelaide (1,600km) or Darwin (1,500km)
data.transport_bus: AAT Kings/Greyhound coaches from Alice Springs (450km, 5hrs, $100-150). Most visitors fly + tour
data.transport_car: From Alice Springs: 450km, 4.5hrs via Stuart/Lasseter Highways. Rental car $80-150/day. Fuel at Erldunda or Yulara
data.visit_duration: 2-3 days (Uluru sunrise, sunset, base walk, Kata Tjuta)
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Sep (cool, dry 20-30°C days, cold nights 5-10°C). Oct-Apr extreme heat 35-45°C (dangerous for walking). Sunrise/sunset essential for color changes
data.detailed_description: Massive sandstone monolith (348m high, 9.4km circumference) in Central Australia. Sacred site to Anangu Aboriginal people who've lived here 30,000+ years. UNESCO World Heritage (cultural). Most striking at sunrise/sunset when rock glows red-orange. Base walk (10.6km, 3hrs) circumnavigates rock with cultural sites. Kata Tjuta (Olgas) - 36 rock domes 50km west, equally impressive. Desert landscape with desert oaks, spinifex grass. Climbing banned October 2019 (was disrespectful to Anangu, dangerous - 35+ deaths). Only resort: Ayers Rock Resort (Yulara) with hotels, campground.
data.history: Formed 550 million years ago from sediment, exposed by erosion. Anangu people sacred site for 30,000+ years, songlines (creation stories) integral to culture. European discovery: William Gosse 1873, named after SA Premier Sir Henry Ayers. Dual naming Ayers Rock/Uluru 1993, officially Uluru 2002. Climbing allowed until 2019 despite Anangu requests to stop. Managed jointly by Anangu and Parks Australia since 1985 handback. UNESCO Heritage 1987 (natural), 1994 (cultural). Tourism began 1950s, resort developed 1980s. 400,000 visitors annually.
tips: Sunrise/sunset viewings essential - rock changes colors dramatically
Base walk (10.6km, 3hrs) best in cool early morning (start 6am)
Bring 3-4 liters water per person - extreme heat, no shade
Respect cultural sites - photography banned at sacred areas (marked)
Stay in Yulara resort village (only option, book months ahead)
Visit Kata Tjuta (50km west) - less crowded, equally spectacular

data.opening_hours: Fiord accessible 24/7. Cruises: multiple departures 9am-3pm. Milford Road (access road) open year-round but avalanche risk winter
data.admission_adult: Scenic cruises $70-150 NZD (~$45-95 USD). Kayaking $145-220. Flightseeing $320-500. No park entry fee
data.admission_notes: Overnight cruises $300-700. Multi-activity combos available. Book online for discounts
data.transport_train: No trains to Milford. Nearest: Queenstown (290km)
data.transport_bus: Coach tours from Queenstown/Te Anau (most common, $80-200 return including cruise). 4.5-5hrs each way
data.transport_car: From Queenstown: 290km, 4hrs via Te Anau. Milford Road scenic but winding. Can close for avalanches in winter. Rental car allows flexibility
data.visit_duration: Full day from Queenstown (including 9hrs driving). Overnight cruise recommended
data.best_time_to_visit: Dec-Feb (summer, warm, longer days, sandflies peak). Shoulder seasons Mar-May, Sep-Nov fewer crowds. Winter Jun-Aug dramatic with snow but road closures. Rainy 200+ days/year - increases waterfalls!
data.detailed_description: Stunning fiord (glacier-carved valley flooded by sea) in Fiordland National Park, UNESCO World Heritage. Sheer cliffs rise 1,200m from dark waters. Mitre Peak (iconic 1,692m pyramid) dominates. Numerous waterfalls: Stirling Falls (155m), Bowen Falls (162m) - flow increases dramatically after rain. Rainforest to sea level. Wildlife: bottlenose dolphins, fur seals, Fiordland crested penguins. Described by Rudyard Kipling as '8th Wonder of World'. Activities: scenic cruises (1.5-2hrs), overnight cruises, kayaking, diving, flightseeing. Accessed via Milford Road - one of world's most scenic drives (119km from Te Anau).
data.history: Carved by glaciers during ice ages, flooded by sea 10,000 years ago. Māori name Piopiotahi after extinct bird. Māori harvested pounamu (greenstone) nearby. Captain Cook sailed past 1770, didn't enter. John Grono entered 1823, named after Milford Haven (Wales). Surveyed 1850s-60s. Homer Tunnel (1.2km, hand-dug 1935-53) enabled road access. Tourism began 1950s after road completed. Now Fiordland National Park (UNESCO 1990). Part of Te Wahipounamu - South West New Zealand World Heritage Area. 600,000+ visitors annually. Wettest inhabited place in NZ (7-9m rain/year).
tips: Rain is NORMAL - enhances waterfalls dramatically (bring waterproof jacket)
Book cruise 1-2 weeks ahead in summer (Dec-Feb)
Drive yourself for flexibility (stop at Mirror Lakes, Chasm, etc en route)
Overnight cruise worth splurge - see fiord at sunset/sunrise, fewer crowds
Check Homer Tunnel status in winter - can close for avalanches
Sandflies INTENSE - bring repellent, cover up

data.opening_hours: Island: 24/7 accessible. Abbey: 9am-7pm (May-Aug), 9:30am-6pm (Sep-Apr). Last entry 1hr before closing
data.admission_adult: Island village FREE. Abbey: €11 (~$12 USD). Night visits (summer): €14
data.admission_child: Abbey free (under 18). EU residents under 26 free
data.admission_notes: Audio guide included. Night visits Jul-Aug with lights/music. First Sunday of month Nov-Mar: free abbey entry
data.transport_train: TGV to Rennes (1hr 20min from Paris, €60-100) or Dol-de-Bretagne (closer). Then bus/taxi to Mont
data.transport_bus: Flixbus Paris-Mont direct (5hrs, €20-40). Local buses from Rennes/St-Malo. Shuttle from parking to Mont (free)
data.transport_car: From Paris: 360km, 3.5hrs via A11/A84. Park 2.5km away (€15/day), free shuttle bus to Mont. No car access to island
data.visit_duration: Half day (3-4 hours). Full day if leisurely
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Jun, Sep (pleasant weather, fewer crowds). Jul-Aug very crowded. Early morning (before 10am) or evening (after 5pm) for fewer tourists. High tide most dramatic (check tide times)
data.detailed_description: Medieval abbey and village on rocky tidal island in Normandy. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Connected to mainland by causeway (submerged at high tide - island becomes isolated). Abbey perched on 92m granite outcrop, founded 708 AD. Gothic-Romanesque architecture, 'Merveille' (Marvel) cloisters. Village below with ramparts, narrow streets, restaurants, shops. One of France's most iconic sites. Tidal range up to 14m - among Europe's highest. At high tide, island surrounded by water. Mont appears to 'rise from sea' - spectacular sight.
data.history: Founded 708 AD when Bishop Aubert built oratory after vision of Archangel Michael. Benedictine abbey established 966 AD. Became pilgrimage site Middle Ages ('Chemin du Mont-Saint-Michel'). Withstood English siege during Hundred Years' War. Used as prison during French Revolution-1863. Restoration began late 1800s. Causeway built 1879 (caused silting). New bridge opened 2014 (allows water flow, reducing silt). Benedictine monks returned 1966. UNESCO Heritage 1979. About 3 million visitors annually. Tides can rise 14m in 6hrs - fastest in Europe after Severn Estuary.
tips: Check tide times - high tide most dramatic, island surrounded by water
Arrive early (before 10am) or late afternoon (after 5pm) to avoid crowds
Park at designated lot (€15/day) - free shuttle to Mont (1km)
Abbey visit takes 1-1.5hrs - worth entrance fee for views
Restaurants inside expensive/touristy - bring picnic or eat outside
Night visits Jul-Aug spectacular with lights/music (book ahead)

data.opening_hours: Daily 7am-8pm (summer), 8am-4pm (winter). Last entry 2hrs before closing. Open year-round
data.admission_adult: 250 HRK (~€33/$35 USD) high season (Jul-Aug), 80 HRK (€11) low season (Nov-Mar), 150 HRK mid-season
data.admission_notes: 2-day tickets available. Price includes boat rides, electric shuttle trains. Online booking required high season. Cash/card accepted
data.transport_train: No direct trains. Train to Zagreb or Zadar, then bus to Plitvice (2-3hrs)
data.transport_bus: Buses from Zagreb (2.5hrs, 100-150 HRK), Zadar (2hrs), Split (4.5hrs). Stops at park entrances
data.transport_car: From Zagreb: 140km, 2hrs via D1. Split: 260km, 3.5hrs. Parking 10 HRK/hr at entrances 1 and 2
data.visit_duration: Half day minimum (4-6hrs). Full day for complete experience
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Jun (waterfalls at peak flow, green foliage, moderate crowds). Sep-Oct (fall colors, fewer tourists). Jul-Aug very crowded. Winter magical with ice but some paths closed
data.detailed_description: UNESCO World Heritage National Park with 16 terraced lakes connected by waterfalls, cascades, caves. Crystal-clear turquoise water from mineral-rich springs. Lakes divided: 12 Upper Lakes (limestone canyon), 4 Lower Lakes (dramatic waterfalls including 78m Veliki Slap - tallest). Wooden boardwalks/pathways over/beside water. Dense forests (beech, fir, spruce) with bears, wolves, lynx, deer. 8 marked trails (2-8hrs). Electric boats cross largest lake (Kozjak). Shuttle trains connect areas. Travertine barriers (mineral deposits) form natural dams - UNESCO recognized ongoing geological process.
data.history: Formed ~12,000-15,000 years ago by water dissolving limestone, creating barriers. Area inhabited since prehistoric times. First hotel built 1896. Became Yugoslavia's first national park 1949. UNESCO Heritage 1979 for unique travertine formations and karst features. During Croatian War of Independence (1991-95), park was front line - occupied, landmines planted (now cleared). Restoration 1995-2000. Now Croatia's most visited park (1.5+ million annually). Over 1,260 plant species, 321 butterflies, 161 bird species. Strict conservation - no swimming (formerly allowed).
tips: Book online for Jul-Aug - entrance limits enforced (7,000/day)
Entrance 1 (lower lakes) easier walking, shorter trails. Entrance 2 (upper lakes) better for long hikes
Visit May-Jun for highest waterfalls (snow melt), fewer crowds than summer
Allow 4-6hrs minimum - rushing defeats the purpose
Wear non-slip shoes - boardwalks can be slippery
No swimming allowed (strict rules) - fines for violations

data.opening_hours: Aurora visible: Sep-Mar (darkness needed). Peak: Dec-Feb. Best viewing: 10pm-2am. Weather and solar activity dependent
data.admission_adult: Tours: $50-150 USD. Self-drive free (just fuel/car rental). Super Jeep tours $150-250. Boat tours $100-180
data.admission_notes: Clear skies essential. Tours refund/reschedule if no aurora. Reykjavik light pollution reduces visibility
data.transport_train: No trains in Iceland
data.transport_bus: Tours include pickup from Reykjavik hotels. Public buses limited at night
data.transport_car: Self-drive rental $50-150/day. Drive to dark areas away from Reykjavik. Check road conditions (road.is)
data.visit_duration: 3-4 hours per viewing attempt. Stay 3-7 nights in Iceland to maximize chances
data.best_time_to_visit: Sep-Oct, Feb-Mar (dark enough, milder weather than Dec-Jan). Peak darkness Dec-Jan but colder. Need 65+ latitude, clear skies, solar activity
data.detailed_description: Aurora Borealis (Northern Lights) - natural light display caused by solar particles colliding with Earth's atmosphere. Appears as dancing green, pink, purple, red curtains of light across night sky. Visible in Arctic regions 65°+ latitude. Iceland ideal location: Sep-Mar darkness, accessible, infrastructure. Best locations: Thingvellir National Park (1hr from Reykjavik), Jökulsárlón Glacier Lagoon (southeast), Akureyri (north), Westfjords. Requires: darkness (10pm-2am), clear skies (no clouds/rain), solar activity (KP index 2+). Not guaranteed - nature dependent.
data.history: Norse mythology: reflections of Valkyrie armor. Indigenous Sami people believed aurora was souls of departed. Scientific explanation discovered 1896 by Kristian Birkeland (Norwegian). Caused by solar wind particles entering Earth's magnetosphere. Colors determined by altitude/gas type: green (oxygen 100-200km), red (oxygen 200km+), blue/purple (nitrogen). Solar activity follows 11-year cycle (next peak ~2025). Iceland tourism boomed 2000s-2010s for aurora viewing. NASA studies aurora from space. Visible ~200+ nights/year in Iceland.
tips: Stay 3-7 nights minimum to maximize chances (weather unpredictable)
Check aurora forecast: vedur.is (Iceland Met Office), KP index apps
Get away from city lights - Reykjavik too bright
Tours offer better success (guides know where to go, track forecasts)
Dress very warm - standing outside for hours in winter (-5 to 5°C)
Bring tripod for photos - long exposure needed (10-30 seconds)

data.opening_hours: Old Town: 24/7 accessible (public). City Walls: 8am-7:30pm (summer), 9am-3pm (winter). Last entry 90min before closing
data.admission_adult: Old Town FREE to walk. City Walls: 250 HRK (~€33/$35). Game of Thrones tour: 200-400 HRK
data.admission_notes: Dubrovnik Card available (250-350 HRK, includes walls + museums + transport). Buy walls ticket early to avoid queues
data.transport_train: No trains to Dubrovnik. Nearest: Split (230km), then bus/ferry
data.transport_bus: Buses from Split (4.5hrs, 120-180 HRK), Zagreb (11hrs), Sarajevo (5hrs). Station 3km from Old Town, local bus #1A/1B to Pile Gate
data.transport_car: From Split: 230km, 3hrs via coastal road. Parking outside Old Town 40-80 HRK/day (limited). No cars allowed in Old Town
data.visit_duration: Full day minimum. 2-3 days to explore properly
data.best_time_to_visit: May-Jun, Sep-Oct (warm, fewer crowds, lower prices). Jul-Aug very crowded (cruise ships, Game of Thrones fans). Avoid midday heat on walls
data.detailed_description: UNESCO World Heritage medieval walled city on Adriatic coast. 'Pearl of the Adriatic' - stunning limestone streets, baroque buildings, orange-tiled roofs. Complete city walls (2km, 25m high) walk offers panoramic views. Main street: Stradun (Placa) - polished limestone, cafes, shops. Key sites: Rector's Palace (Gothic-Renaissance), Sponza Palace, Orlando Column, Cathedral. Fort Lovrijenac (42m cliff outside walls). Famous as King's Landing filming location (Game of Thrones). Cable car to Mount Srđ (412m) for sunset views. Beaches: Banje (near Old Town), Sveti Jakov.
data.history: Founded 7th century by refugees from Epidaurum. Became wealthy republic 'Ragusa' (1358-1808) rivaling Venice - maritime trade power. Golden age 15th-16th centuries. Baroque reconstruction after 1667 earthquake. Napoleon abolished republic 1808. Austria-Hungary ruled 1815-1918, then Yugoslavia. Croatian War of Independence (1991-92): heavily shelled by Serbian/Montenegrin forces (over 2,000 shells hit Old Town). UNESCO listed 1979, 'endangered' 1991-98 during war, restored. Game of Thrones filming 2011-17 brought tourism boom. Now Croatia's top destination (1.3M cruise passengers/year).
tips: Walk City Walls early (8am) or late afternoon - avoid midday heat/crowds
Walls circuit takes 1.5-2hrs - bring water, no shade, lots of steps
Enter Old Town via Pile Gate (west, main entrance) or Ploce Gate (east)
Cable car to Mt Srđ at sunset for best views (220 HRK return)
Game of Thrones tours popular but Old Town recognizable without guide
Avoid cruise ship days (check port schedule) - 3-4 ships = 10,000 people
facilities: Hotels (luxury in Old Town, budget outside)

data.opening_hours: Daily 8:30am-6pm (Mar-Nov), 8:30am-5:30pm (Dec-Feb). Last entry 90min before closing
data.admission_adult: ¥120 (~$17 USD) Mar-Nov, ¥150 Dec-Feb (peak season). Includes all pits + museum
data.admission_child: Half price with student ID. Free under 1.2m height or under 16 with ID
data.admission_notes: Buy online to skip queues. Audio guide ¥30. Guided tours available. No photography in some areas
data.transport_train: High-speed train from Beijing (4.5hrs, ¥515), Shanghai (6hrs, ¥516). Xi'an station or North station
data.transport_bus: Tourist buses 306, 307 from Xi'an Railway Station (1hr, ¥7). Private tour buses available
data.transport_car: From Xi'an city center: 40km, 1hr. Taxis ¥100-150. Parking available at site
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May, Sep-Nov (mild weather 15-25°C, fewer crowds). Avoid Oct 1-7 (National Holiday), Chinese New Year. Weekday mornings quieter
data.detailed_description: Archaeological site with 8,000+ life-size terracotta warrior and horse statues buried with China's First Emperor Qin Shi Huang (210 BCE). UNESCO World Heritage Site. Three main excavation pits: Pit 1 (largest, 6,000 warriors in battle formation), Pit 2 (cavalry, archers), Pit 3 (command headquarters). Each warrior unique - different faces, ranks, hairstyles. Originally painted in bright colors (faded). Bronze weapons found. Emperor's actual tomb unexcavated (200m away). Museum displays restored warriors, weapons, chariots. Discovered 1974 by local farmers digging well.
data.history: Created 246-210 BCE for Emperor Qin Shi Huang's afterlife protection. Estimated 700,000 workers built tomb complex over 38 years. Buried with emperor 210 BCE, forgotten for 2,000+ years. Rediscovered March 1974 by farmers Yang Zhifa and brothers. Excavations began immediately. UNESCO Heritage 1987. Over 8,000 soldiers, 130 chariots, 670 horses uncovered. Estimated 6,000+ still buried. Colors deteriorate within minutes of exposure to air - preservation challenge. Bronze weapons showed chromium coating (anti-rust technology lost until 1950s). Now one of world's most important archaeological sites. 7+ million visitors annually.
tips: Go early (8:30am opening) to avoid tour group rush (peak 10am-2pm)
Start with Pit 1 (most impressive), then Pit 3, Pit 2, Museum
Audio guide (¥30) worthwhile for historical context
Allow 2-3 hours minimum - don't rush this once-in-lifetime site
Photography allowed but no flash. Some special exhibitions prohibit photos
Combine with Emperor Qin's tomb mound (5min drive, less impressive)

data.opening_hours: Daily 6am-5pm. Sunrise access: 4:30-6am (separate ticket, limited). Temple illumination: 6:30-8:30pm
data.admission_adult: $25 USD (foreigners). 50,000 IDR (Indonesians). Sunrise ticket: $47
data.admission_notes: Prambanan combo ticket available. Dress code: shoulders/knees covered. Sarongs provided if needed
data.transport_train: Train to Yogyakarta (from Jakarta 8hrs, Surabaya 5hrs). Then bus/taxi to Borobudur (1.5hrs, 40km)
data.transport_bus: Buses from Yogyakarta Jombor Terminal (1.5hrs, 40,000 IDR). Direct tourist shuttles available
data.transport_car: From Yogyakarta: 40km, 1.5hrs. Taxis 300,000-400,000 IDR return. Parking at temple
data.best_time_to_visit: Dry season Apr-Oct (less rain). Sunrise visits magical but book weeks ahead (limited 150 people/day). Early morning 6-8am cooler, fewer crowds
data.detailed_description: World's largest Buddhist temple and monument - 9th-century Mahayana Buddhist temple in Central Java. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Massive pyramid structure: 9 stacked platforms (6 square, 3 circular), topped by central dome. Contains 2,672 relief panels (life of Buddha), 504 Buddha statues. Designed as 3D mandala - represents Buddhist cosmology. Built 750-850 CE from 2 million stone blocks. Abandoned 14th century after decline of Buddhism in Java, rediscovered 1814. Volcanic ash and jungle covered it for centuries. Restored 1970s-80s by UNESCO.
data.history: Built during Sailendra Dynasty reign around 800 CE under King Samaratungga. Took 75 years to construct with 2 million andesite stone blocks. Abandoned ~1400s after Islam spread to Java and volcanic eruptions (nearby Mt Merapi). Forgotten under volcanic ash and jungle. British Lt. Governor Thomas Stamford Raffles rediscovered 1814. Major restoration 1907-11 (Dutch), 1973-83 (UNESCO $25M project). UNESCO Heritage 1991. Survives earthquakes due to interlocking stones (no mortar). Threatened by volcanic activity (Mt Merapi 25km away). 3-4 million visitors annually.
tips: Sunrise tickets ($47) book 2-4 weeks ahead - limited 150/day, magical experience
Standard visit: arrive 6-7am for cooler temps, fewer crowds
Dress code strictly enforced: knees/shoulders covered (free sarongs available)
Climb to top level (3 circular terraces) for best views
Combine with Prambanan Hindu temple (1hr away) - combo tickets available
Avoid weekends and Indonesian holidays (extremely crowded)
facilities: Limited wheelchair access (ground level only, no climbing)

data.opening_hours: Observatory: 10am-11pm weekdays, 10am-midnight weekends. Last entry 30min before closing. Open daily
data.admission_adult: ₩16,000 (~$12 USD) observatory. Cable car: ₩11,000 round-trip. Combo: ₩21,000
data.admission_notes: Online booking discounts. Combination tickets available. Restaurant reservations separate
data.transport_train: Subway Line 3/4 to Chungmuro or Myeongdong. Then cable car or 20min uphill walk to base
data.transport_bus: Bus 02, 05 to Namsan Cable Car station. Yellow circular bus to tower base
data.transport_car: Limited parking at Namsan Park lots. Cable car recommended. Parking ₩3,000-5,000
data.best_time_to_visit: Sunset (5-7pm) for day and night views. Clear days year-round (spring/fall best). Weekdays less crowded than weekends. Winter illumination spectacular
data.detailed_description: Iconic 236m communication and observation tower (N Seoul Tower) atop 243m Namsan Mountain - total 479m above sea level. Seoul's symbol since 1980. Opened to public 1980 as observation tower. Rotating restaurant, digital observatory with 360° Seoul views. Famous for 'Locks of Love' - thousands of padlocks attached to fences by couples. Illuminated nightly with LED shows (color changes by season/events). Clear days: see as far as Incheon. Namsan Park surrounding tower offers hiking trails, Seoul City Wall. Cable car from base (or 20min walk up). Renovated 2005, renamed N Seoul Tower.
data.history: Built 1969-1971 as Korea's first integrated transmission tower for TV/radio broadcasting. Originally off-limits to public (transmission facility). Opened as tourist observatory 1980. Became Seoul icon 1980s-90s. Renovated 2005 by CJ Corporation, rebranded 'N Seoul Tower' ('N' for Namsan, New, Nature). LED lighting system added 2005. Love locks tradition started 2000s, inspired by Paris bridges. Featured in numerous K-dramas (My Love from the Star, City Hunter) boosting tourism. Now 8+ million visitors annually. Part of Namsan Park (Seoul's green lung).
tips: Sunset timing perfect for day views + night illumination
Take cable car up ($8 round-trip) - scenic, saves energy
Walk down through Namsan Park trails for free city views
Love locks area free photo op (bring your own lock if participating)
Skip rotating restaurant (overpriced) - just observatory sufficient
Myeongdong shopping district 10min walk from cable car base

data.opening_hours: Daily 9:30am-5pm (last entry 4:30pm). Open year-round except Holi festival
data.admission_adult: ₹700 (~$8.50 USD) for foreigners (includes audio guide). ₹200 Indians
data.admission_notes: Still residence of Jaipur royal family - private quarters off-limits. Video camera extra ₹200. Photography allowed
data.transport_train: Jaipur Junction Railway Station (3km, 15min by auto-rickshaw ₹100-150)
data.transport_bus: City buses to City Palace. Auto-rickshaw most convenient from anywhere in Jaipur
data.transport_car: From airport: 13km, 30min. Parking available near palace. Auto-rickshaw ₹200-300 from hotel areas
data.best_time_to_visit: Oct-Mar (cool, dry 10-25°C). Avoid Apr-Jun extreme heat 40-45°C. Morning 9:30-11am cooler, fewer crowds. Combine with nearby Hawa Mahal, Jantar Mantar
data.detailed_description: Magnificent royal residence complex in Jaipur's Pink City, still home to current Maharaja. Built 1729-32 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II. Blend of Rajasthani and Mughal architecture. Key sections: Mubarak Mahal (welcome palace, textile museum), Chandra Mahal (7-story royal residence, top floors still private), Diwan-i-Khas (private audience hall, two giant silver urns - world's largest), Diwan-i-Am (public audience hall). Peacock Gate - stunning painted gateway. Museums display royal costumes, weapons, manuscripts, miniature paintings. City Palace still hosts royal ceremonies.
data.history: Built 1729-32 when Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II moved capital from Amber to newly founded Jaipur. Expanded by successive rulers through 20th century. Combines Rajput, Mughal, European architectural styles. Served as administrative and ceremonial seat of Jaipur state 1727-1949. After Indian independence, became museum while royal family retained private quarters. Two giant silver urns (each 345kg, 4.5ft tall) made 1902 for Maharaja Madho Singh II to carry Ganges water to England. Chandra Mahal's top floors still royal residence (current Maharaja Sawai Padmanabh Singh). UNESCO consideration ongoing.
tips: Audio guide included in foreigner ticket - use it for historical context
Visit early morning (9:30am) before tour groups arrive
Dress modestly (shoulders/knees covered) - it's still royal residence
Photography allowed but be respectful - royal family lives here
Combine with Hawa Mahal (5min walk), Jantar Mantar (10min walk) - save day
Silver urns in Diwan-i-Khas are world's largest - don't miss
facilities: Wheelchair accessible (ground floor, limited upper levels)

data.opening_hours: Daily 8am-1pm, 2pm-5pm. Closed Tuesdays and religious holidays. Check before visiting
data.admission_adult: 1,000 BTN (~$12 USD) entrance fee. Must be with licensed Bhutan tour operator
data.admission_notes: Can only visit Bhutan with licensed tour ($250-300/day Sustainable Development Fee includes accommodation, meals, guide, transport). Independent travel not allowed
data.transport_train: No trains to Bhutan. Fly to Paro Airport (only international airport)
data.transport_bus: No public transport. All tourists travel with tour operator vehicles
data.transport_car: Tour operator provides vehicle from Paro town (12km, 30min to trailhead). Hiking from trailhead 6-7km round-trip
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-May (spring, rhododendrons bloom, clear views). Sep-Nov (fall, post-monsoon, clear skies). Avoid Jun-Aug monsoon (rain, leeches, poor visibility)
data.detailed_description: Sacred Taktsang Monastery (Paro Taktsang) clinging to cliff 900m above Paro Valley. Built 1692 around cave where Guru Rinpoche meditated 3 months in 8th century, bringing Buddhism to Bhutan. One of Bhutan's holiest sites. Perched at 3,120m elevation on cliff face. Hike: 6-7km round-trip, 900m elevation gain, 4-5 hours total. Steep switchbacks through pine forest. Cafeteria halfway point (2hrs up). Temple complex: meditation caves, chapels, monks' quarters. Prayer flags, prayer wheels throughout. Stunning valley views. Can only visit Bhutan with licensed tour operator ($250-300/day fee).
data.history: Legend: Guru Rinpoche (Padmasambhava) flew from Tibet on tigress's back, meditated in cave 3 months (747 CE), subdued demons, brought Buddhism to Bhutan. Cave became pilgrimage site. Monastery built 1692 by Tenzin Rabgye (4th Drgaon Dzongpo). Expanded over centuries. Destroyed by fire 1998, reconstructed 2005 (exact replica). Still active monastery with ~30 monks. Sacred site - pilgrims climb barefoot. Bhutan opened to tourism 1974 with strict quotas. Sustainable Development Fee ensures low-impact, high-value tourism.
tips: Must book licensed Bhutan tour months ahead ($250-300/day mandatory fee)
Start hike early (7-8am) - takes 2-3hrs up, 1.5-2hrs down
Moderate fitness required - steep, 900m elevation gain at altitude
Bring water, snacks (cafeteria at halfway point but limited)
Dress modestly (long pants, covered shoulders) - active monastery
No photography inside temples - respect monks, sacred space

data.opening_hours: Upper Canyon: 8am-4pm (summer extended). Lower Canyon: 8am-4pm. Closed Dec-Jan (low season). Weather dependent (flash flood risk)
data.admission_adult: $80-100 USD (Upper), $50-70 (Lower) includes Navajo permit + guide. Photography tours $120-200
data.admission_child: $40-50 (ages 5-12). Free under 5 (Lower only, Upper min age 8)
data.admission_notes: Must visit with authorized Navajo guide - no independent access. Book 2-4 weeks ahead in peak season. Photography tours require tripod permit
data.transport_train: No trains. Nearest: Flagstaff (150mi, 2.5hrs drive)
data.transport_bus: No public transport. Tours from Flagstaff/Sedona available (full day)
data.transport_car: From Flagstaff: 135 miles, 2.5hrs via US-89. From Las Vegas: 280mi, 4.5hrs. Page, AZ base town (10min drive to canyon). Parking at tour offices
data.visit_duration: 1-1.5 hours per canyon (Upper or Lower). Full day if doing both + Horseshoe Bend
data.best_time_to_visit: Mar-Oct (open season). Light beams: late Mar-early Oct, best 11am-1pm. Apr-May, Sep-Oct fewer crowds. Avoid Jul-Aug monsoon (flash floods, closures)
data.detailed_description: Stunning slot canyon on Navajo land carved by flash floods through Navajo sandstone. Two sections: Upper Canyon (Tsé bighánílíní 'place where water runs through rocks') - ground-level entry, famous for light beams shining through narrow openings (Mar-Oct, midday). Lower Canyon (Hazdistazí 'spiral rock arches') - requires climbing stairs/ladders, less crowded, longer, more challenging. Narrow passages (2-3m wide), smooth wave-like walls in orange, red, purple hues. Can only visit with authorized Navajo guide (sacred land). Photography permits required for tripods. Flash flood danger - closes in rain.
data.history: Carved over millions of years by seasonal monsoon flash floods eroding Navajo sandstone. Sacred to Navajo Nation. Discovered by Navajo shepherd in 1931 while searching for lost sheep. Remained relatively unknown until 1990s when photographers popularized light beam images. Opened to tourism 1997. Managed by Navajo Nation. Flash floods dangerous - 11 tourists died 1997 in Lower Canyon during sudden flood. Now strict safety protocols, weather monitoring. Upper Canyon most photographed slot canyon in world. Featured in countless films, commercials, Windows XP wallpaper. 280,000+ visitors annually.
tips: Book 2-4 weeks ahead for peak season (Apr-May, Sep-Oct)
Light beams (Upper): only Mar-Oct, best 11am-1pm (but most crowded)
Lower Canyon less crowded, longer (1.5hrs), requires moderate fitness (ladders)
Photography tours ($120-200) allow tripods, more time - worth it for photographers
Combine with Horseshoe Bend (5min drive) for full day
Flash flood risk - tours cancel if rain forecast (refunded)
facilities: Upper: wheelchair accessible (smooth sand). Lower: NOT accessible (stairs/ladders)

data.opening_hours: Park: 24/7 year-round. Visitor centers: 9am-5pm daily. Gondola/attractions: vary by season (typically 9am-9pm summer)
data.admission_adult: CAD $11.50/day or CAD $23.50/3-day vehicle pass. Annual Discovery Pass: CAD $75.25
data.admission_notes: National park pass mandatory for all vehicles. Banff Gondola extra CAD $72. Lake Louise parking: reservation required peak season
data.transport_train: VIA Rail to Jasper (north), then bus to Banff. Rocky Mountaineer luxury train (seasonal, expensive)
data.transport_bus: Brewster Express/Roam Transit from Calgary Airport (2hrs, CAD $70-100). Greyhound discontinued
data.transport_car: From Calgary: 130km, 1.5hrs via Trans-Canada Hwy 1. Rental car recommended for flexibility. Parking reservations required at Lake Louise peak season
data.visit_duration: 3-5 days minimum. 7+ days for Jasper combination
data.best_time_to_visit: Jun-Sep (summer, all roads/trails open, warm 15-25°C). Jul-Aug peak crowds. Sep shoulder season ideal (fall colors, fewer people). Dec-Mar skiing. May, Oct shoulder (some closures, fewer crowds)
data.detailed_description: Canada's first national park (1885) in Canadian Rockies - 6,641 km² of mountains, glaciers, forests, lakes. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Main town: Banff. Iconic sites: Lake Louise (turquoise glacial lake, Fairmont Chateau), Moraine Lake (Valley of Ten Peaks, $20 bill photo), Peyto Lake (blue), Bow Lake. Activities: hiking, wildlife viewing (elk, bears, bighorn sheep), hot springs (Banff Upper Hot Springs CAD $9), canoeing, skiing (3 ski resorts). Icefields Parkway (230km scenic drive to Jasper). Gondola to Sulphur Mountain summit (2,281m). Over 1,600km hiking trails.
data.history: Established 1885 as Canada's first national park after railway workers discovered hot springs 1883. Originally called 'Banff Hot Springs Reserve' (26 km²). Named after Banffshire, Scotland (birthplace of Canadian Pacific Railway president). Expanded to current size early 1900s. Trans-Canada Highway completed 1962 improved access. UNESCO Heritage 1984 (Canadian Rocky Mountain Parks). Wildlife overpasses built 1990s-2000s (35+ structures) - reduced collisions 80%. Now 4+ million visitors annually. Parks Canada manages ecological preservation vs tourism balance.
tips: Lake Louise parking: MUST book reservation online peak season (Jun-Sep)
Stay in Canmore (cheaper, less crowded) and day trip to Banff
Hike Lake Agnes Tea House (8km round-trip from Lake Louise) - iconic
Moraine Lake sunrise magical but arrive by 5:30am (parking fills by 6am)
Wildlife: maintain 30m from elk/deer, 100m from bears (serious fines)
Icefields Parkway to Jasper (230km) one of world's most scenic drives
